He and the other officials go to Hezekiah to report the commander's words (2 Kings 18:37)
The king sends Eliakim to Isaiah the son of Amoz (2 Kings 19:2)
He delivers the message that 'this is a day of distress, rebuke, and disgrace' (2 Kings 19:3)
events.significance: He served as the vital link between the royal court and the prophetic word.

events.title: The Other Eliakim
events.summary: Centuries later, the name is given to a son of Josiah who is made king by Pharaoh Neco.
developments: Pharaoh Neco makes Eliakim king in place of Josiah (2 Kings 23:34)
Pharaoh changes his name to Jehoiakim (2 Kings 23:34)
This Eliakim reigns as a vassal king and does evil in God's sight (2 Kings 23:34-37)
The name change signified Egyptian dominance over the Judean throne (2 Kings 23:34)
events.significance: The later bearer of the name represents the decline and foreign subjugation of Judah.

events.title: The Messianic Title
events.summary: In the New Testament, the authority given to Eliakim is transferred to Jesus Christ.
developments: Jesus addresses the church in Philadelphia (Revelation 3:7)
He identifies Himself as the one 'who has the key of David' (Revelation 3:7)
The phrase 'who opens and no one will shut' is taken directly from Eliakim's prophecy (Revelation 3:7)
This validates the typological role of Eliakim as a foreshadowing of the Messiah's authority (Isaiah 22:22)
events.significance: Eliakim’s administrative role became a permanent part of the church’s understanding of Jesus' power.

label: Eliashib
overview: Eliashib was the High Priest of Jerusalem during the time of Nehemiah and the reconstruction of the city's walls. He is initially praised for his leadership in rebuilding the Sheep Gate, setting an example for the rest of the community. However, his legacy is later complicated by his close ties to Sanballat and Tobiah, the primary enemies of Nehemiah's reforms. Eliashib controversially provided Tobiah with a large storage room in the Temple courts and allowed his grandson to marry Sanballat's daughter. His life reflects the ongoing tension between the priestly establishment and the rigorous spiritual reforms required after the exile.

events.title: Leadership in Construction
events.summary: Eliashib is the first person named in the collaborative effort to rebuild the walls of Jerusalem.
developments: Eliashib the high priest rises up with his brothers the priests (Nehemiah 3:1)
They build the Sheep Gate and consecrate it (Nehemiah 3:1)
They build as far as the Tower of the Hundred and the Tower of Hananel (Nehemiah 3:1)
The High Priest's personal involvement provided a spiritual mandate for the project (Nehemiah 3:1)
events.significance: This was a high point of his ministry, showing active support for the restoration.

events.title: Compromise with Tobiah
events.summary: While Nehemiah is away, Eliashib forms an alliance with one of the nation's chief enemies.
developments: Eliashib was appointed over the chambers of the house of God (Nehemiah 13:4)
He is described as being 'related to Tobiah' (Nehemiah 13:4)
He prepares a large chamber for Tobiah in the Temple (Nehemiah 13:5)
The room had previously been used for grain offerings and frankincense (Nehemiah 13:5)
events.significance: His actions defiled the sacred space and undermined Nehemiah's authority.

events.title: The Conflict with Nehemiah
events.summary: Nehemiah returns to find the Temple misused and forcibly corrects Eliashib’s decision.
developments: Nehemiah returns from the king of Babylon (Nehemiah 13:6)
He is very angry when he learns of the room provided for Tobiah (Nehemiah 13:7-8)
He throws all of Tobiah's household furniture out of the chamber (Nehemiah 13:8)
Nehemiah commands the chambers to be cleansed and restored to holy use (Nehemiah 13:9)
events.significance: This event highlight the corruption that had seeped into the high priesthood.

events.title: Marital Intermarriage
events.summary: Eliashib’s family ties with the enemies of Jerusalem lead to the expulsion of his grandson.
developments: One of the sons of Joiada, son of Eliashib the high priest, was the son-in-law of Sanballat (Nehemiah 13:28)
Sanballat the Horonite was a leading adversary of the Jews (Nehemiah 13:28)
Nehemiah chases the grandson away from his presence (Nehemiah 13:28)
The incident is cited as a defilement of the priesthood and the covenant of the Levites (Nehemiah 13:29)
events.significance: The high priest's household became a symbol of spiritual compromise through worldly alliances.

label: Elihu
overview: Elihu, the son of Barachel the Buzite, was the fourth and youngest speaker in the Book of Job, appearing after the three friends had failed to silence Job. He remained silent through the initial debates out of respect for his elders, but eventually spoke up when his anger was kindled by their ineffective arguments and Job’s self-justification. Elihu’s discourse differs from the others by emphasizing God’s sovereignty and the idea that suffering can be a form of divine discipline or a warning to prevent further sin. While he is not mentioned in the final divine rebuke, he serves as a transition between the limited wisdom of man and the majestic revelation of God from the whirlwind.

events.title: The Silent Witness
events.summary: Elihu is introduced as a younger bystander who has been listening to the debate with growing frustration.
developments: He is identified as the son of Barachel the Buzite of the family of Ram (Job 32:2)
His anger burns against Job for justifying himself rather than God (Job 32:2)
His anger burns against the three friends because they found no answer (Job 32:3)
He waited to speak because the others were older than him (Job 32:4)
events.significance: Elihu represents the voice of a new generation challenging established but failed traditions.

events.title: Claiming Divine Inspiration
events.summary: Elihu begins his speech by asserting that wisdom comes from the Spirit, not just old age.
developments: He declares that the spirit in man and the breath of the Almighty gives understanding (Job 32:8)
He says he is 'full of words' and the spirit within him constrains him (Job 32:18)
He invites Job to answer him, positioning himself as a mediator for God (Job 33:5-7)
He claims his words are from a heart of uprightness and knowledge (Job 33:3)
events.significance: He shifts the source of authority from human experience to divine revelation.

events.title: Suffering as Instruction
events.summary: Elihu argues that God uses pain and dreams to turn man away from pride and destruction.
developments: He states that God speaks in one way and then in another, though man does not perceive it (Job 33:14)
He suggests God uses dreams to seal instruction and hide pride from man (Job 33:15-17)
He describes a person chastened with pain on a bed to keep them from the pit (Job 33:19-22)
He speaks of a 'mediator' who can declare man's uprightness and bring restoration (Job 33:23-26)
events.significance: This introduced the concept of 'pedagogical' or corrective suffering into the narrative.

events.title: Defense of God's Justice
events.summary: Elihu rebukes Job for suggesting that God is unfair and insists that the Almighty cannot do wrong.
developments: He quotes Job's claim of innocence and God's perceived enmity (Job 34:5-6)
He declares, 'Far be it from God that he should do wickedness' (Job 34:10)
He argues that God pays every man according to his work (Job 34:11)
He asserts that God's sovereignty over all life ensures his justice (Job 34:13-15)
events.significance: Elihu reinforces the traditional view of God's character while making it more dynamic.

events.title: The Greatness of God
events.summary: Elihu emphasizes that human righteousness does not benefit God, nor does sin harm Him.
developments: He asks Job if his righteousness is more than God's (Job 35:2)
He points to the clouds and sky to show God's distance and transcendence (Job 35:5)
He explains that God does not answer the 'cry of the wicked' because of their pride (Job 35:12-13)
He encourages Job to magnify God's work, which men have beheld (Job 36:24)
events.significance: He prepares Job for God's appearance by refocusing on the vastness of the Creator.

events.title: The Storm Approaching
events.summary: Elihu describes the power of God in nature as a physical storm literally approaches the scene.
developments: He speaks of the thunder as the voice of God (Job 37:2-5)
He describes the snow, rain, and whirlwind as tools of divine will (Job 37:6-13)
He tells Job to 'stop and consider the wondrous works of God' (Job 37:14)
He concludes that the Almighty is 'excellent in power' and 'great in justice' (Job 37:23)
events.significance: Elihu’s final words set the stage for God to speak from the whirlwind in the next chapter.

label: Elijah
overview: Elijah the Tishbite was one of the most powerful and significant prophets in Israel's history, known for his fierce defense of Yahweh worship against the Baals of Jezebel. His ministry was marked by spectacular miracles, including controlling the rain, multiplying food for a widow, and calling down fire from heaven on Mount Carmel. Elijah was a man of intense passion who experienced both the heights of spiritual victory and the depths of personal despair. Instead of dying a natural death, he was taken to heaven in a whirlwind by a chariot of fire, leaving his mantle to his successor, Elisha. Elijah remains a central figure in biblical prophecy, with his 'return' being linked to the arrival of the Messiah.

events.title: The Drought Announcement
events.summary: Elijah abruptly appears before King Ahab to announce a supernatural drought as judgment for idolatry.
developments: He is identified as Elijah the Tishbite, of the settlers in Gilead (1 Kings 17:1)
He declares there shall be no dew or rain except by his word (1 Kings 17:1)
God commands him to hide by the Brook Cherith (1 Kings 17:2-3)
Ravens bring him bread and meat every morning and evening (1 Kings 17:4-6)
events.significance: This act initiated a direct power struggle between the God of Israel and Baal.

events.title: Ministry in Zarephath
events.summary: During the famine, Elijah is sent to a Phoenician widow and performs miracles of provision and resurrection.
developments: The jar of flour and jug of oil do not run dry for the widow (1 Kings 17:14-16)
The widow's son falls ill and stops breathing (1 Kings 17:17)
Elijah stretches himself over the child three times and cries to God (1 Kings 17:21)
The Lord listens to Elijah and the child's life returns (1 Kings 17:22)
events.significance: These miracles proved that Elijah’s God had authority even in the heart of Baal's territory.

events.title: The Showdown on Mount Carmel
events.summary: Elijah challenges 450 prophets of Baal to a contest of fire to prove who is the true God.
developments: Elijah mocks the prophets of Baal when their god fails to answer (1 Kings 18:27)
He repairs the altar of the Lord and drenches it with water (1 Kings 18:30-35)
God sends fire that consumes the sacrifice, the wood, the stones, and the dust (1 Kings 18:38)
The people confess 'The Lord, he is God' and execute the false prophets (1 Kings 18:39-40)
events.significance: This was the definitive spiritual victory of Elijah's career, breaking the hold of Baal on the people.

events.title: The Return of Rain
events.summary: Elijah prays persistently on the mountain until the three-year drought is finally broken.
developments: Elijah tells Ahab to go up and eat, for there is the sound of rushing rain (1 Kings 18:41)
He bows on the earth and sends his servant seven times to look at the sea (1 Kings 18:42-43)
A small cloud like a man's hand appears (1 Kings 18:44)
Elijah runs before Ahab's chariot to the gates of Jezreel (1 Kings 18:45-46)
events.significance: The miracle of the rain confirmed Elijah's authority over the natural elements.

events.title: Flight to Horeb
events.summary: Threatened by Jezebel, Elijah flees into the wilderness and experiences a deep emotional collapse.
developments: He sits under a broom tree and asks God to take his life (1 Kings 19:4)
An angel feeds him for a forty-day journey to Mount Horeb (1 Kings 19:5-8)
Elijah tells God he is the only faithful prophet left (1 Kings 19:10)
God speaks to him not in wind, earthquake, or fire, but in a 'low whisper' (1 Kings 19:11-13)
events.significance: This encounter taught Elijah that God's work continues even in quietness and through others.

events.title: Commissioning Successors
events.summary: God gives Elijah new instructions to anoint kings and a prophetic replacement.
developments: He is told to anoint Hazael as king of Syria and Jehu as king of Israel (1 Kings 19:15-16)
He is commanded to anoint Elisha as prophet in his place (1 Kings 19:16)
Elijah finds Elisha plowing and throws his mantle on him (1 Kings 19:19)
Elisha leaves everything to follow and serve Elijah (1 Kings 19:20-21)
events.significance: This ensured the continuity of the prophetic movement beyond Elijah's individual life.

events.title: Rebuke over Naboth's Vineyard
events.summary: Elijah confronts Ahab in the stolen vineyard to announce the doom of his dynasty.
developments: Ahab asks, 'Have you found me, O my enemy?' (1 Kings 21:20)
Elijah predicts that dogs will lick Ahab's blood in the place they licked Naboth's (1 Kings 21:19)
He declares that Jezebel will be eaten by dogs (1 Kings 21:23)
Ahab humbles himself, leading God to delay the judgment (1 Kings 21:27-29)
events.significance: Elijah acted as the moral judge over royal abuse of power.

events.title: Fire on the Captains
events.summary: Elijah calls down fire on the soldiers of King Ahaziah who attempt to arrest him.
developments: King Ahaziah sends fifty men to seize Elijah (2 Kings 1:9)
Fire from heaven consumes the first two groups of fifty (2 Kings 1:10-12)
The third captain begs for mercy and Elijah goes with him (2 Kings 1:13-15)
Elijah delivers a death sentence to the king for consulting Baal-zebub (2 Kings 1:16)
events.significance: This reinforced the holy and dangerous nature of the prophetic office.

events.title: Ascension to Heaven
events.summary: Elijah is miraculously taken from the earth without dying.
developments: He travels from Gilgal to Bethel to Jericho with Elisha (2 Kings 2:1-4)
He strikes the Jordan with his mantle and the waters part (2 Kings 2:8)
A chariot of fire and horses of fire separate him from Elisha (2 Kings 2:11)
Elijah goes up by a whirlwind into heaven (2 Kings 2:11)
events.significance: Elijah is one of only two men in the Bible to enter heaven without experiencing death.

events.title: Prophecy of Return
events.summary: The Old Testament ends with a promise that Elijah will return before the Day of the Lord.
developments: God says, 'Behold, I will send you Elijah the prophet' (Malachi 4:5)
His mission is to turn the hearts of fathers to children and vice versa (Malachi 4:6)
This return is associated with preparing the way for the Lord (Malachi 3:1)
This prophecy creates a lasting Jewish and Christian expectation (Matthew 11:14)
events.significance: Elijah became the herald of the Messianic age.

events.title: The Transfiguration
events.summary: Elijah appears with Moses and Jesus on the mountain to discuss Jesus' coming departure.
developments: Peter, James, and John see Elijah and Moses in glory (Luke 9:30-31)
They talk with Jesus about His 'exodus' which he was to accomplish at Jerusalem (Luke 9:31)
The disciples ask about the scribes saying Elijah must come first (Matthew 17:10)
Jesus identifies John the Baptist as the 'Elijah' who has already come (Matthew 17:11-13)
events.significance: Elijah’s appearance validated Jesus as the fulfillment of the Prophets.

label: Elimelech
overview: Elimelech was an Israelite from the tribe of Judah and the husband of Naomi, living in Bethlehem during the time of the Judges. Faced with a severe famine, he made the consequential decision to move his family—his wife and two sons—to the pagan land of Moab. This journey, intended to be temporary, ended in tragedy as Elimelech died in Moab, leaving his family in a foreign land. His death, followed by the deaths of his two sons, created the state of emptiness and desolation that Naomi would carry back to Bethlehem. Elimelech’s life serves as the catalyst for the narrative of Ruth, highlighting the vulnerability of the family line and the need for a kinsman-redeemer.

events.title: Life in Bethlehem
events.summary: Elimelech is introduced as an Ephrathite of high standing in the town of Bethlehem.
developments: He is a man of the tribe of Judah (Ruth 1:1-2)
The name Elimelech means 'My God is King' (Ruth 1:2)
He lives during the era when the judges ruled (Ruth 1:1)
He has a wife, Naomi, and two sons, Mahlon and Chilion (Ruth 1:2)
events.significance: His family represents the core of the covenant community in Judah.

events.title: Decision to Sojourn
events.summary: In response to a famine in the land, Elimelech decides to move his household to Moab.
developments: A famine strikes the land of Israel (Ruth 1:1)
Elimelech takes his family to 'sojourn' in the country of Moab (Ruth 1:1)
Moab was a traditional enemy of Israel, making the move spiritually risky (Ruth 1:1)
The family settles in the plains of Moab (Ruth 1:2)
events.significance: His choice to leave the Promised Land for food set a tragic trajectory for his family.

events.title: Death in a Foreign Land
events.summary: Elimelech dies shortly after the move, leaving Naomi as a widow with two young sons.
developments: The text states simply, 'Elimelech, the husband of Naomi, died' (Ruth 1:3)
His death occurs before his sons had married (Ruth 1:3-4)
Naomi is left to raise the children in Moabite culture (Ruth 1:3)
This death was the first of three major losses for the household (Ruth 1:3-5)
events.significance: The loss of the patriarch in a foreign land was a catastrophic event for the family's future.

events.title: Legacy of Property
events.summary: Despite his death, Elimelech's ownership of land in Bethlehem remains a key legal factor.
developments: Naomi returns to Bethlehem and still has a right to Elimelech's field (Ruth 4:3)
Boaz is identified as a 'relative on her husband's side' (Ruth 2:1)
He belongs to the clan of Elimelech (Ruth 2:1)
Ruth gleans in the field of a man from Elimelech’s extended family (Ruth 2:3)
events.significance: His name and property rights provide the framework for the redemption story.

events.title: The Redemption of the Name
events.summary: At the city gate, Boaz ensures that Elimelech’s name is not blotted out from Israel.
developments: Boaz publicly states he has bought all that belonged to Elimelech (Ruth 4:9)
He marries Ruth specifically to 'perpetuate the name of the dead' (Ruth 4:10)
The community elders witness the legal transfer (Ruth 4:11)
The son born to Boaz and Ruth is seen as a restorer of life for Elimelech's house (Ruth 4:14-17)
events.significance: The narrative concludes with Elimelech’s line being restored and leading to King David.

label: Eliphaz
overview: Eliphaz the Temanite was the most prominent and respected of Job’s three friends who came to console him during his affliction. Representing the wisdom of age and tradition, Eliphaz argued that God is perfectly just and that suffering is always a result of personal sin. He famously claimed to have received a mystical night vision emphasizing man’s impurity before God. While he initially spoke with some gentleness, he eventually accused Job of specific, heinous crimes to justify his theological framework. In the end, God rebuked Eliphaz for misrepresenting Him and required him to offer sacrifices and seek Job’s intercession.

events.title: The Seven-Day Silence
events.summary: Eliphaz and his companions arrive to comfort Job, sitting in shocked silence for a full week.
developments: He is identified as Eliphaz the Temanite (Job 2:11)
The friends make an appointment to condole and comfort Job (Job 2:11)
He weeps and sprinkles dust on his head upon seeing Job's condition (Job 2:12)
He sits on the ground for seven days and seven nights without speaking (Job 2:13)
events.significance: This initial period showed the depth of his genuine friendship and grief.

events.title: The First Argument
events.summary: Eliphaz breaks the silence by suggesting that the innocent never perish and Job must have sinned.
developments: He asks who that was innocent ever perished (Job 4:7)
He claims those who sow trouble reap the same (Job 4:8)
He recounts a terrifying night vision where a spirit spoke to him (Job 4:12-16)
He concludes that man is born to trouble as the sparks fly upward (Job 5:7)
events.significance: Eliphaz established the foundational 'orthodoxy' of the debate: suffering equals guilt.

events.title: The Second Argument
events.summary: Increasingly frustrated, Eliphaz rebukes Job for his 'unwise' and 'impious' words.
developments: He asks if a wise man should answer with windy knowledge (Job 15:2)
He accuses Job of doing away with the fear of God (Job 15:4)
He claims Job's own mouth condemns him (Job 15:6)
He describes the miserable life and end of the wicked man (Job 15:20-35)
events.significance: The tone shifts from corrective to accusatory as Job refuses to confess.

events.title: Specific Accusations
events.summary: In his final speech, Eliphaz invents crimes to explain the severity of Job’s loss.
developments: He asks if God cares for Job's righteousness (Job 22:2-3)
He accuses Job of stripping the naked of their clothing (Job 22:6)
He claims Job has given no water to the weary and withheld bread (Job 22:7)
He urges Job to 'Agree with God, and be at peace' (Job 22:21)
events.significance: This represents the peak of human presumption, assuming guilt based on external circumstances.

events.title: Divine Rebuke
events.summary: After God speaks from the whirlwind, He addresses Eliphaz as the leader of the friends.
developments: The Lord says to Eliphaz, 'My anger burns against you and against your two friends' (Job 42:7)
God declares that Eliphaz has not spoken of Him what is right (Job 42:7)
He is commanded to take seven bulls and seven rams for a burnt offering (Job 42:8)
God specifies that Job must pray for them for their offering to be accepted (Job 42:8)
events.significance: This was the ultimate theological correction, proving that Eliphaz's 'wisdom' was folly.

events.title: The Humble Sacrifice
events.summary: Eliphaz obeys the Lord, leading to his reconciliation and a final lesson in grace.
developments: Eliphaz the Temanite goes and does what the Lord told him (Job 42:9)
The Lord accepts Job's prayer on Eliphaz's behalf (Job 42:9)
This act of submission restored the friends to God's favor (Job 42:9-10)
Eliphaz witnesses the doubling of Job's prosperity (Job 42:10-12)
events.significance: His story ends with the humbling of the wise man before the mystery of God.

label: Elisha
overview: Elisha, the son of Shaphat, was the successor to the prophet Elijah and one of the most prolific miracle-workers in the Old Testament. Called from his oxen to follow Elijah, he requested and received a 'double portion' of his mentor's spirit. His long ministry was characterized by a deep involvement in the political and personal lives of both Israelites and foreigners, performing acts ranging from parting the Jordan to raising a boy from the dead and healing the Aramean general Naaman. Elisha’s miracles often emphasized God’s care for the individual and His sovereign control over international affairs. He remained a spiritual powerhouse until his death, leaving a legacy that influenced Israel for generations.

events.title: The Call of Elisha
events.summary: Elijah finds Elisha plowing in a field and symbolically calls him to the prophetic life.
developments: Elisha is plowing with twelve yoke of oxen (1 Kings 19:19)
Elijah passes by him and casts his mantle upon him (1 Kings 19:19)
Elisha slaughters the oxen and burns the equipment to feed the people (1 Kings 19:21)
He departs and follows Elijah as his assistant (1 Kings 19:21)
events.significance: This total abandonment of his former life marked his absolute commitment to God's service.

events.title: Inheriting the Spirit
events.summary: As Elijah is taken to heaven, Elisha receives the mantle and the authority of his master.
developments: He refuses to leave Elijah, traveling with him to the Jordan (2 Kings 2:2-6)
He asks for a 'double portion' of Elijah's spirit (2 Kings 2:9)
He sees the chariot of fire and cries, 'My father, my father!' (2 Kings 2:12)
He picks up the fallen mantle and strikes the Jordan, parting the waters (2 Kings 2:13-14)
events.significance: The miracle at the Jordan confirmed to the other prophets that Elijah's spirit rested on Elisha.

events.title: Miracles of Compassion
events.summary: Elisha performs a series of miracles to provide for the poor and the grieving.
developments: He multiplies a widow's oil to pay her debts (2 Kings 4:1-7)
He prophesies that a barren Shunammite woman will have a son (2 Kings 4:14-17)
When the boy dies, Elisha prays and life returns to him (2 Kings 4:32-35)
He purifies a pot of poisonous stew for the sons of the prophets (2 Kings 4:38-41)
events.significance: Elisha’s ministry was frequently focused on the domestic needs of the faithful remnant.

events.title: The Healing of Naaman
events.summary: Elisha heals a powerful Aramean commander, showing that God's power extends beyond Israel.
developments: Naaman comes with horses and chariots to Elisha's door (2 Kings 5:9)
Elisha sends a messenger telling him to wash seven times in the Jordan (2 Kings 5:10)
Naaman is cleansed of his leprosy and acknowledges the God of Israel (2 Kings 5:14-15)
Elisha refuses any payment for the miracle (2 Kings 5:16)
events.significance: This event was a key witness to the surrounding nations of Yahweh’s supremacy.

events.title: War with Syria
events.summary: Elisha acts as a spiritual intelligence officer, frustrating the King of Syria’s plans.
developments: He warns the King of Israel about Aramean ambushes (2 Kings 6:8-12)
He sees a mountain full of horses and chariots of fire protecting him (2 Kings 6:17)
He strikes the Aramean army with blindness and leads them to Samaria (2 Kings 6:18-20)
He advises the King of Israel to feed the enemies and release them (2 Kings 6:22-23)
events.significance: Elisha demonstrated that spiritual vision is more powerful than military strength.

events.title: Deliverance from Famine
events.summary: During a desperate siege, Elisha prophesies a sudden and miraculous end to starvation.
developments: He predicts that fine flour and barley will be cheap the next day (2 Kings 7:1)
A royal official mocks the prophecy (2 Kings 7:2)
God causes the Arameans to hear a great army and flee (2 Kings 7:6-7)
The city is saved and the mocking official is trampled at the gate (2 Kings 7:16-20)
events.significance: The fulfillment of his word vindicated Elisha as a true prophet during a national crisis.

events.title: Anointing of Jehu
events.summary: Elisha initiates the violent end of the house of Ahab by anointing a new king.
developments: Elisha calls a young prophet to go to Ramoth-gilead (2 Kings 9:1)
The young prophet pours oil on Jehu and declares him king (2 Kings 9:6)
Jehu is commissioned to strike down the entire house of Ahab (2 Kings 9:7-10)
This fulfilled the long-standing prophecy given to Elijah (2 Kings 9:36)
events.significance: Elisha was the administrative agent who brought about the promised judgment on the Omride dynasty.

events.title: Final Prophecy and Death
events.summary: Even on his deathbed, Elisha remains a source of military guidance for Israel.
developments: King Jehoash of Israel weeps over the dying prophet (2 Kings 13:14)
Elisha has the king shoot the 'arrow of the Lord's victory' (2 Kings 13:15-17)
He rebukes the king for only striking the ground three times (2 Kings 13:18-19)
Elisha dies and is buried (2 Kings 13:20)
events.significance: His life ended with a final emphasis on the need for bold, persistent faith.

events.title: Posthumous Miracle
events.summary: A dead man is restored to life after his body touches Elisha’s bones.
developments: Moabite raiders invade the land during a burial (2 Kings 13:20)
The burial party throws the man's corpse into Elisha's grave (2 Kings 13:21)
As soon as the man touched the bones of Elisha, he revived and stood up (2 Kings 13:21)
This unique event emphasized the enduring power of Elisha's spirit (2 Kings 13:21)
events.significance: It served as a final sign that God’s life-giving power remained in Israel.

events.title: Referenced by Jesus
events.summary: Jesus uses Elisha’s healing of Naaman to challenge the exclusivity of the people in Nazareth.
developments: Jesus notes there were many lepers in Israel in the time of Elisha (Luke 4:27)
He points out that none were cleansed except Naaman the Syrian (Luke 4:27)
This teaching enraged the locals who tried to kill Jesus (Luke 4:28-29)
Jesus validates Elisha as a prophet to all people (Luke 4:27)
events.significance: Elisha’s ministry was a prototype for the global reach of the Gospel.

label: Elizabeth
overview: Elizabeth was the wife of the priest Zechariah and a relative of Mary, the mother of Jesus. Descended from the line of Aaron, she was described as righteous and blameless, yet she suffered the social stigma of lifelong barrenness. Her life was transformed in her old age when she miraculously conceived John the Baptist, the forerunner of the Messiah. Elizabeth was the first to recognize Mary as the 'mother of my Lord,' and her joyful greeting became a key part of the Nativity narrative. Her story highlights God’s power to remove shame and His meticulous preparation for the arrival of Christ.

events.title: Heritage and Character
events.summary: Elizabeth is introduced as a woman of priestly descent and exceptional piety.
developments: She is from the daughters of Aaron (Luke 1:5)
She and Zechariah are righteous before God (Luke 1:6)
They walked blamelessly in all the commandments of the Lord (Luke 1:6)
They had no child because Elizabeth was barren and both were old (Luke 1:7)
events.significance: Her profile mirrors the great matriarchs of the Old Testament who faced similar trials.

events.title: The Promise to Zechariah
events.summary: While Elizabeth remains at home, an angel announces to her husband that she will conceive.
developments: Gabriel tells Zechariah, 'Your wife Elizabeth will bear you a son' (Luke 1:13)
He is told to name the child John (Luke 1:13)
The child is promised to be filled with the Spirit from the womb (Luke 1:15)
Zechariah is struck mute because of his unbelief in the message (Luke 1:18-20)
events.significance: Elizabeth’s future son was divinely designated as the herald of the Messiah.

events.title: The Miraculous Conception
events.summary: Elizabeth conceives in her old age and spends several months in seclusion.
developments: After these days his wife Elizabeth conceived (Luke 1:24)
She kept herself hidden for five months (Luke 1:24)
She says, 'The Lord has looked on me, to take away my reproach' (Luke 1:25)
Her pregnancy was the 'sign' given to Mary by the angel (Luke 1:36)
events.significance: Her pregnancy was the first physical evidence of the new era of salvation.

events.title: The Visitation of Mary
events.summary: Mary travels to the hill country of Judah to visit Elizabeth, leading to a prophetic encounter.
developments: As Mary enters, the baby leaps in Elizabeth's womb (Luke 1:41)
Elizabeth is filled with the Holy Spirit (Luke 1:41)
She cries out, 'Blessed are you among women!' (Luke 1:42)
She asks, 'Why is this granted to me that the mother of my Lord should come to me?' (Luke 1:43)
events.significance: Elizabeth provided the first human recognition of Jesus’ identity.

events.title: Birth of John the Baptist
events.summary: Elizabeth gives birth and insists on the name John, defying family expectations.
developments: The time comes for Elizabeth to give birth and she has a son (Luke 1:57)
Neighbors and relatives rejoice with her (Luke 1:58)
On the eighth day, she declares, 'No; he shall be called John' (Luke 1:60)
Zechariah confirms the name on a tablet and regains his speech (Luke 1:63-64)
events.significance: Elizabeth’s obedience in naming the child was essential to fulfilling the angelic command.

label: Elkanah
overview: Elkanah was a Levite from the hill country of Ephraim and the father of the prophet Samuel. A man of deep devotion, he faithfully traveled to Shiloh every year to worship and offer sacrifices at the Tabernacle. Elkanah is primarily known for his tender relationship with his wife Hannah, whom he loved dearly despite her barrenness. He supported her through her years of sorrow and eventually rejoiced with her when God answered her prayer for a son. Elkanah’s life exemplifies the quiet faithfulness of the Israelite household during the transition from the era of the Judges to the Monarchy.

events.title: Family and Vocation
events.summary: Elkanah is introduced as an inhabitant of Ramathaim-zophim with two wives.
developments: He is the son of Jeroham, from the hill country of Ephraim (1 Samuel 1:1)
His wives are named Hannah and Peninnah (1 Samuel 1:2)
He is an Ephraimite by residence but a Levite by lineage (1 Chronicles 6:22-28)
Peninnah had children, but Hannah had none (1 Samuel 1:2)
events.significance: His family situation set the stage for the birth of Israel's greatest prophet.

events.title: Yearly Worship at Shiloh
events.summary: Elkanah maintains a strict routine of national worship at the central sanctuary.
developments: He goes up from his city year by year (1 Samuel 1:3)
He goes to worship and sacrifice to the Lord of hosts (1 Samuel 1:3)
He gives portions of the sacrifice to his family (1 Samuel 1:4)
He gives a 'double portion' to Hannah because he loved her (1 Samuel 1:5)
events.significance: His ritual consistency provided a spiritual foundation for his household.

events.title: Compassion for Hannah
events.summary: Elkanah attempts to comfort Hannah during her intense seasons of weeping and fasting.
developments: Hannah weeps and will not eat because of her barrenness and Peninnah's mockery (1 Samuel 1:7)
Elkanah asks, 'Hannah, why do you weep? And why do you not eat?' (1 Samuel 1:8)
He asks, 'Am I not more to you than ten sons?' (1 Samuel 1:8)
His love for her was independent of her ability to bear children (1 Samuel 1:5)
events.significance: He represents a rare biblical example of a husband prioritizing his wife's emotional well-being over social expectations.

events.title: The Birth of Samuel
events.summary: God remembers Hannah, and Elkanah becomes the father of the promised son.
developments: The couple returns home to Ramah and Elkanah knows his wife (1 Samuel 1:19)
The Lord remembered her and she bore a son (1 Samuel 1:19-20)
They name the child Samuel (1 Samuel 1:20)
Elkanah continues his yearly sacrifice after the birth (1 Samuel 1:21)
events.significance: The arrival of Samuel was the divine answer to the family's long-standing grief.

events.title: Agreement to the Vow
events.summary: Elkanah supports Hannah’s decision to dedicate Samuel to the service of the Lord.
developments: Hannah decides to stay home until the child is weaned (1 Samuel 1:22)
Elkanah says, 'Do what seems best to you; wait until you have weaned him' (1 Samuel 1:23)
He adds, 'Only, may the Lord establish his word' (1 Samuel 1:23)
This support was necessary to make the religious vow legally binding (Numbers 30:6-8)
events.significance: His willingness to give up his firstborn son showed his supreme loyalty to God.

events.title: Dedication at Shiloh
events.summary: Elkanah and Hannah travel to the Tabernacle to formally hand Samuel over to Eli.
developments: They bring the child along with a large sacrifice (1 Samuel 1:24-25)
They present Samuel to Eli the priest (1 Samuel 1:25)
Elkanah worships the Lord there (1 Samuel 1:28)
Elkanah returns to his house in Ramah while the boy remains (1 Samuel 2:11)
events.significance: This act completed the transition of Samuel into his prophetic calling.

events.title: Blessing of more Children
events.summary: Eli blesses Elkanah for his sacrifice, and the family is granted more offspring.
developments: Eli blesses Elkanah and his wife each year (1 Samuel 2:20)
He prays that the Lord give them offspring in place of the boy they gave (1 Samuel 2:20)
The Lord visits Hannah and she bears three sons and two daughters (1 Samuel 2:21)
Elkanah sees the full restoration of his household (1 Samuel 2:21)
events.significance: Elkanah’s faithfulness resulted in a legacy of abundance and divine favor.

label: Elon
overview: Elon the Zebulunite was a judge of Israel who led the nation for ten years during the period recorded in the Book of Judges. His tenure followed the judgeship of Ibzan and preceded that of Abdon. While the biblical account of Elon is brief, it notes that he was buried in Aijalon in the land of Zebulun, suggesting his influence was primarily centered in the northern tribal territories. Elon is categorized as one of the 'minor judges' whose relatively peaceful and stable leadership helped maintain the order of the tribes between the more famous periods of crisis and military deliverance.

events.title: Rise to Leadership
events.summary: Elon is appointed as the judge of Israel following the death of Ibzan.
developments: He is identified as Elon the Zebulunite (Judges 12:11)
He rose to leadership in a time of relative calm (Judges 12:11)
His authority covered the tribal confederation of Israel (Judges 12:11)
He was the tenth named judge in the book (Judges 12:11)
events.significance: His rise ensured the continuity of legal and social order in Israel.

events.title: Ten Years of Rule
events.summary: Elon’s term of service lasts for one decade, providing stability to the nation.
developments: He judged Israel for ten years (Judges 12:11)
The text records no major wars or oppressions during this time (Judges 12:11)
He maintained the religious and civil laws of the Mosaic covenant (Judges 12:11)
His rule bridged the gap between the eras of the eastern and southern judges (Judges 12:8-13)
events.significance: The length of his rule contributes to the chronological structure of the pre-monarchy period.

events.title: Death and Burial
events.summary: Elon dies after his ten-year tenure and is buried in his ancestral territory.
developments: Elon the Zebulunite died (Judges 12:12)
He was buried at Aijalon in the land of Zebulun (Judges 12:12)
This burial site was distinct from the Aijalon in the south (Judges 12:12)
His burial marked the transition to the next judge, Abdon (Judges 12:13)
events.significance: His burial in his own land signifies the peaceful conclusion of his life's work.

events.title: The Name in History
events.summary: The name Elon also appears in early patriarchal history as a Hittite relative of Esau.
developments: Esau marries Basemath the daughter of Elon the Hittite (Genesis 26:34)
He is also mentioned as the father of Adah, another wife of Esau (Genesis 36:2)
The name suggests a commonality across different ethnic groups in Canaan (Genesis 26:34)
The name means 'Oak' or 'Terebinth' in Hebrew (Genesis 26:34)
events.significance: The recurrence of the name shows its standing within the region's ancient culture.

label: Elymas Bar Jesus
overview: Elymas, also known as Bar-Jesus, was a Jewish false prophet and sorcerer in the court of the Roman proconsul Sergius Paulus on the island of Cyprus. During the first missionary journey of Paul and Barnabas, Elymas aggressively attempted to turn the proconsul away from the faith to maintain his own influence. The Apostle Paul, filled with the Holy Spirit, denounced him as a 'son of the devil' and an enemy of all righteousness, striking him with temporary blindness. This dramatic demonstration of God's power over magic led to the immediate conversion of the proconsul and established Paul’s authority in his new mission.

events.title: Court Magician in Paphos
events.summary: Elymas is introduced as a prominent figure in the entourage of the Roman governor in Cyprus.
developments: He is identified as a Jewish false prophet named Bar-Jesus (Acts 13:6)
He was with the proconsul Sergius Paulus, an intelligent man (Acts 13:7)
The name 'Elymas' is given as the meaning of his title as a magician (Acts 13:8)
He held a position of significant political and spiritual influence (Acts 13:7-8)
events.significance: He represented the syncretism of Jewish identity and pagan occultism.

events.title: Opposition to the Gospel
events.summary: When the proconsul seeks to hear the word of God, Elymas actively intervenes to stop him.
developments: Sergius Paulus summons Barnabas and Saul to hear the word (Acts 13:7)
Elymas the magician opposes them (Acts 13:8)
He seeks to turn the proconsul away from the faith (Acts 13:8)
His resistance was a direct challenge to the apostolic mission (Acts 13:8)
events.significance: This was the first recorded high-level spiritual conflict on Paul's missionary journey.

events.title: Paul's Denunciation
events.summary: Paul, filled with the Spirit, exposes Elymas's true nature and motives.
developments: Paul looks intently at him and calls him 'full of all deceit and villainy' (Acts 13:9-10)
He labels him a 'son of the devil' and an 'enemy of all righteousness' (Acts 13:10)
He asks, 'Will you not stop making crooked the straight paths of the Lord?' (Acts 13:10)
The rebuke identifies Elymas as a spiritual saboteur (Acts 13:10)
events.significance: Paul used apostolic authority to publicly unmask the false prophet.

events.title: Judgment of Blindness
events.summary: Paul pronounces a supernatural sentence on Elymas that is immediately fulfilled.
developments: Paul says, 'The hand of the Lord is upon you, and you will be blind' (Acts 13:11)
He specifies the blindness will be 'for a time' (Acts 13:11)
Immediately mist and darkness fall upon him (Acts 13:11)
Elymas goes about seeking people to lead him by the hand (Acts 13:11)
events.significance: The physical blindness of Elymas mirrored his internal spiritual condition.

events.title: The Proconsul's Conversion
events.summary: The defeat of Elymas leads directly to the conversion of the high-ranking Roman official.
developments: The proconsul sees what has occurred (Acts 13:12)
He believes in the message of the Lord (Acts 13:12)
He is astonished at the teaching of the Lord (Acts 13:12)
The power of the gospel is proven superior to the power of magic (Acts 13:12)
events.significance: Elymas's downfall served as the definitive sign for the first major Gentile convert of the journey.

label: Enoch
overview: Enoch was a patriarch of the seventh generation from Adam, known as the man who 'walked with God.' Unlike his ancestors and descendants whose lives were summarized by their deaths, Enoch’s life ended uniquely: 'God took him,' and he did not experience physical death. The brief account in Genesis is expanded in the New Testament, where he is described as a man of faith who pleased God and a prophet who warned his generation of the coming divine judgment. Enoch stands as a powerful symbol of intimacy with the Creator and the hope of eternal life beyond mortality.

events.title: The Other Enoch
events.summary: A different Enoch is mentioned as the firstborn son of Cain, associated with the birth of human civilization.
developments: Cain knew his wife and she bore Enoch (Genesis 4:17)
Cain builds a city and names it after his son Enoch (Genesis 4:17)
This line is characterized by technological development and escalating violence (Genesis 4:18-24)
The name Enoch means 'dedicated' or 'initiated' (Genesis 4:17)
events.significance: This Enoch represents the earthly, humanistic line of development apart from God.

events.title: Birth in the Sethite Line
events.summary: The righteous Enoch is born into the lineage of Seth, the seventh generation from Adam.
developments: He is the son of Jared (Genesis 5:18)
Jared lived 162 years before fathering Enoch (Genesis 5:18)
He is part of the genealogy that preserved the worship of the Lord (Genesis 5:1-32)
He is born into a world of increasing longevity but also increasing sin (Genesis 5:21)
events.significance: Enoch represents the spiritual peak of the pre-flood patriarchal line.

events.title: Walking with God
events.summary: Enoch’s life is defined by a deep and consistent fellowship with his Creator.
developments: Enoch lived sixty-five years and fathered Methuselah (Genesis 5:21)
After the birth of Methuselah, he walked with God for 300 years (Genesis 5:22)
He fathered other sons and daughters during this time (Genesis 5:22)
The phrase 'walked with God' is used twice to emphasize his character (Genesis 5:22-24)
events.significance: This established the biblical ideal of a life characterized by divine communion.

events.title: The Translation
events.summary: Enoch is removed from the earth by God, bypassing the universal experience of death.
developments: Enoch's total lifespan was 365 years (Genesis 5:23)
The text says, 'Enoch walked with God, and he was not' (Genesis 5:24)
The reason for his disappearance is simply stated: 'for God took him' (Genesis 5:24)
This makes him the first human to ever escape the curse of death (Genesis 5:24)
events.significance: His departure provided the first hint of hope for life after the Fall.

events.title: The Commendation of Faith
events.summary: The New Testament interprets Enoch’s removal as a reward for his unwavering faith.
developments: By faith Enoch was taken up so that he should not see death (Hebrews 11:5)
He was not found, because God had taken him (Hebrews 11:5)
Before he was taken, he was commended as having pleased God (Hebrews 11:5)
He is used to prove that without faith it is impossible to please Him (Hebrews 11:6)
events.significance: Enoch became the theological proof that faith is the prerequisite for divine favor.

events.title: The Prophecy of Judgment
events.summary: Enoch is identified as a prophet who predicted the Lord's return for judgment.
developments: Enoch is called 'the seventh from Adam' (Jude 1:14)
He prophesied about the Lord coming with ten thousands of His holy ones (Jude 1:14)
His message focused on executing judgment on the ungodly (Jude 1:15)
He specifically addressed the 'ungodly deeds' and 'harsh things' spoken by sinners (Jude 1:15)
events.significance: Enoch is revealed to be the first prophet of the Second Coming of Christ.

label: Epaphras
overview: Epaphras was a faithful minister and a 'fellow servant' of the Apostle Paul, credited with founding the church in Colossae. Likely converted during Paul’s lengthy stay in Ephesus, he took the gospel back to his home region in the Lycus Valley, establishing congregations in Colossae, Hierapolis, and Laodicea. Epaphras was known for his intense prayer life, described as 'wrestling' in prayer for the spiritual maturity and stability of the believers. He eventually joined Paul in Roman imprisonment, where he continued to advocate for the churches he loved. Epaphras represents the vital role of local evangelists in the organic spread of the early Christian faith.

events.title: Founder of the Colossian Church
events.summary: Epaphras is identified as the person through whom the Colossians first heard and learned the gospel.
developments: Paul notes the Colossians learned the grace of God from Epaphras (Colossians 1:7)
He is called a 'beloved fellow servant' (Colossians 1:7)
He is described as a 'faithful minister of Christ' on behalf of the church (Colossians 1:7)
He brought Paul the report of the Colossians' love in the Spirit (Colossians 1:8)
events.significance: Epaphras was the primary apostolic link for a church Paul had never personally visited.

events.title: The Ministry of Prayer
events.summary: Paul highlights the deep spiritual labor Epaphras performs through intercession.
developments: He is identified as 'one of you,' indicating his Colossian origin (Colossians 4:12)
He is described as always 'struggling' or 'wrestling' on their behalf in prayer (Colossians 4:12)
His specific prayer goal is that the believers stand mature and fully assured (Colossians 4:12)
Paul bears witness to Epaphras’s hard work for those in Hierapolis and Laodicea (Colossians 4:13)
events.significance: Epaphras serves as the biblical model for the strenuous nature of intercessory prayer.

events.title: Fellow Prisoner in Rome
events.summary: During Paul's imprisonment, Epaphras stays with him, sharing in his bonds for the gospel.
developments: Paul sends greetings from Epaphras to Philemon (Philemon 1:23)
He explicitly calls him 'my fellow prisoner in Christ Jesus' (Philemon 1:23)
His presence provided Paul with companionship and direct news of the Lycus Valley (Philemon 1:23)
He is listed among the core team of fellow workers in Rome (Philemon 1:24)
events.significance: His willingness to share Paul's imprisonment demonstrated his absolute loyalty to the mission.

label: Epaphroditus
overview: Epaphroditus was a member of the church in Philippi who was sent by the congregation to bring a financial gift to the Apostle Paul in prison. He served not only as a messenger but also as a personal attendant to Paul, ministering to his physical and emotional needs. During his stay in Rome, Epaphroditus became dangerously ill and nearly died, causing great distress to both Paul and the Philippian church. Upon his recovery, Paul sent him back to Philippi with high commendation, describing him as a 'brother,' 'fellow worker,' and 'fellow soldier.' Epaphroditus exemplifies the sacrificial and risky service of laypeople in supporting the work of the apostles.

events.title: The Philippian Messenger
events.summary: Epaphroditus is dispatched by his home church to provide relief and support to Paul.
developments: Paul calls him 'your messenger and minister to my need' (Philippians 2:25)
He carries a financial contribution from the Philippians to Rome (Philippians 4:18)
His mission was to act as a substitute for the church's direct service (Philippians 2:30)
He is described as a 'fellow soldier' in the cause of the gospel (Philippians 2:25)
events.significance: He was the physical bridge of love and support between the church and the apostle.

events.title: Near-Fatal Illness
events.summary: While serving Paul in Rome, Epaphroditus suffers a health crisis that brings him to the brink of death.
developments: He was ill and 'near to death' (Philippians 2:27)
His sickness was likely linked to the strain of his service or travel (Philippians 2:30)
The Philippian church heard he was ill, which caused him great distress (Philippians 2:26)
God had mercy on him and on Paul, preventing a double sorrow (Philippians 2:27)
events.significance: The event highlights the physical risks taken by early Christian missionaries and travelers.

events.title: The Return to Philippi
events.summary: Paul decides to send Epaphroditus home early to ease the anxiety of the Philippian believers.
developments: Paul sends him back so the church may rejoice at seeing him (Philippians 2:28)
Paul urges the church to receive him in the Lord with all joy (Philippians 2:29)
He tells them to 'honor such men' as Epaphroditus (Philippians 2:29)
His return likely included delivering the letter to the Philippians (Philippians 1-4)
events.significance: Epaphroditus’s return served as a living testimony of God’s mercy and Paul’s gratitude.

events.title: A Fragrant Offering
events.summary: Paul uses sacrificial language to describe the gift Epaphroditus delivered.
developments: Paul acknowledges receiving full payment and more from Epaphroditus (Philippians 4:18)
He calls the gift 'a fragrant offering, a sacrifice acceptable and pleasing to God' (Philippians 4:18)
The role of Epaphroditus is linked to the spiritual worship of the church (Philippians 4:18)
This confirms that practical service to ministers is viewed as worship by God (Philippians 4:18-19)
events.significance: He was the agent of a transaction that Paul viewed in high priestly terms.

label: Ephraim
overview: Ephraim was the second son of Joseph and Asenath, born in Egypt during the years of plenty. Though the younger brother, he received the primary blessing from his grandfather Jacob, who prophetically placed him ahead of his older brother Manasseh. As the ancestral head of one of the most powerful tribes of Israel, Ephraim occupied the central hill country of Canaan and became the leading tribe of the Northern Kingdom. In prophetic literature, the name 'Ephraim' is often used as a synonym for the entire northern nation, frequently associated with both its initial strength and its later tragic slide into idolatry and exile.

events.title: Birth in Egypt
events.summary: Ephraim is born to Joseph and Asenath during the seven years of abundance.
developments: He is the younger brother of Manasseh (Genesis 41:51-52)
Joseph names him Ephraim, meaning 'Fruitful' (Genesis 41:52)
Joseph declares, 'God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction' (Genesis 41:52)
He is born into the highest levels of Egyptian nobility through his father (Genesis 41:41-45)
events.significance: His name became a prophecy of the tribe's future dominance and population.

events.title: The Crossed-Hand Blessing
events.summary: Jacob bypasses birthright traditions to give the greater blessing to the younger Ephraim.
developments: Joseph brings his sons to the dying Jacob for a blessing (Genesis 48:1-13)
Jacob intentionally crosses his hands, placing his right hand on Ephraim (Genesis 48:14)
Joseph tries to correct him, but Jacob refuses (Genesis 48:17-19)
Jacob prophesies that Ephraim's offspring shall become a 'multitude of nations' (Genesis 48:19)
events.significance: This act established Ephraim's future preeminence over the other Josephite tribes.

events.title: Allotment of the Hill Country
events.summary: The tribe of Ephraim receives a strategic and fertile portion of central Canaan.
developments: Their border is defined from the Jordan at Jericho to the Great Sea (Joshua 16:1-3)
They receive the region containing the city of Shechem and the mountains (Joshua 16:5-8)
They fail to completely drive out the Canaanites in Gezer (Joshua 16:10)
This central location made them the heart of the tribal confederation (Joshua 16:1-10)
events.significance: Ephraim’s land became the geographic and political center of gravity for Israel.

events.title: Tribal Pride and Conflict
events.summary: The Ephraimites frequently challenge other leaders, asserting their right to be at the center of military affairs.
developments: They criticize Gideon for not calling them to the first part of the war (Judges 8:1)
They later threaten Jephthah for the same perceived slight (Judges 12:1)
Jephthah engages them in a civil war where 42,000 Ephraimites fall (Judges 12:4-6)
They are identified by their inability to pronounce the word 'Shibboleth' (Judges 12:6)
events.significance: Their arrogance often caused internal instability within the nation.

events.title: Leading the Rebellion
events.summary: Jeroboam, an Ephraimite, leads the ten tribes in a revolt against the house of David.
developments: Jeroboam is identified as an Ephraimite of Zeredah (1 Kings 11:26)
He leads the northern tribes in complaining against Solomon's labor and taxes (1 Kings 12:3-4)
The ten tribes secede and make the Ephraimite Jeroboam their king (1 Kings 12:20)
Shechem in the hill country of Ephraim becomes the first northern capital (1 Kings 12:25)
events.significance: Ephraim became the foundational and dominant power of the Northern Kingdom.

events.title: Prophetic Indictment and Lament
events.summary: The prophet Hosea uses 'Ephraim' as a primary name for the unfaithful northern nation.
developments: God rebukes 'Ephraim' for mixing with the nations and being like a 'silly dove' (Hosea 7:8-11)
The tribe is accused of being joined to idols (Hosea 4:17)
God expresses deep grief, asking 'How can I give you up, O Ephraim?' (Hosea 11:8)
The name represents the tragic fall from divine favor into Assyrian exile (Hosea 9:3)
events.significance: Ephraim serves as a biblical symbol of potential greatness lost through spiritual adultery.

label: Erastus
overview: Erastus was a faithful associate of the Apostle Paul who served the early church in both administrative and missionary capacities. He is mentioned as being sent by Paul with Timothy into Macedonia, and later as the city treasurer of Corinth—a position of significant civic importance. His high-ranking secular role indicates that the gospel had reached influential levels of Roman society. Paul includes Erastus’s greetings in the letter to the Romans, and in his final letter to Timothy, he notes that Erastus remained in Corinth, highlighting his long-term commitment to that city’s spiritual life.

events.title: Mission to Macedonia
events.summary: Paul sends Erastus as an assistant to Timothy to prepare the way for his travel through Greece.
developments: Paul stays in Asia for a season (Acts 19:22)
He sends two of his helpers, Timothy and Erastus, into Macedonia (Acts 19:22)
Erastus acts as a trusted emissary for the apostle’s strategy (Acts 19:22)
This mission preceded the great riot in Ephesus (Acts 19:23)
events.significance: Erastus was part of the inner circle of Paul’s mobile missionary team.

events.title: The City Treasurer
events.summary: While Paul is writing from Corinth, Erastus is identified by his high-ranking government office.
developments: Paul includes a list of greetings at the end of his letter (Romans 16:21-23)
He identifies Erastus as 'the city treasurer' (Romans 16:23)
The term used (oikonomos) suggests he managed the financial affairs of Corinth (Romans 16:23)
His greeting was sent alongside that of Quartus, a 'brother' (Romans 16:23)
events.significance: Erastus is a primary example of a high-status professional who integrated his faith with his public office.

events.title: Faithfulness in Corinth
events.summary: In his final letter, Paul records that Erastus has returned to or stayed in his home city.
developments: Paul provides news of his traveling companions (2 Timothy 4:10-20)
He states simply, 'Erastus remained at Corinth' (2 Timothy 4:20)
This was written during Paul’s final imprisonment in Rome (2 Timothy 4:6-7)
It confirms Erastus’s continued residence and likely ministry in Corinth (2 Timothy 4:20)
events.significance: He remained a stable and constant figure in the Pauline network until the very end.

label: Esau
overview: Esau was the firstborn son of Isaac and Rebekah, the twin brother of Jacob, and the ancestor of the nation of Edom. Known as a skillful hunter and a 'man of the field,' he was his father's favorite but famously demonstrated a lack of spiritual discernment by selling his birthright for a bowl of red stew. This impulsive act, followed by the loss of his father’s primary blessing to Jacob's deception, fueled a lifelong bitterness and a vow of revenge. While Esau eventually reconciled with Jacob in a dramatic meeting years later, his legacy in Scripture is that of a 'profane' man who valued immediate physical gratification over eternal spiritual inheritance.

events.title: Birth and the Oracle
events.summary: Esau is born first, covered in red hair, following a struggle with his brother in the womb.
developments: The Lord tells Rebekah the older shall serve the younger (Genesis 25:23)
The first son comes out red and hairy like a garment (Genesis 25:25)
They name him Esau (Genesis 25:25)
He grows up to be a skillful hunter and a man of the field (Genesis 25:27)
events.significance: His physical characteristics and vocational choice defined his rugged, worldly identity.

events.title: Selling the Birthright
events.summary: In a moment of intense hunger, Esau trades his ancestral inheritance for a single meal.
developments: Esau returns from the field exhausted and famished (Genesis 25:29)
He asks Jacob for some of that 'red stew' (Genesis 25:30)
Jacob demands his birthright in exchange for the food (Genesis 25:31)
Esau says, 'I am about to die; of what use is a birthright to me?' (Genesis 25:32)
The text notes that Esau 'despised his birthright' (Genesis 25:34)
events.significance: This act revealed Esau’s focus on the temporary over the eternal.

events.title: Grief of the Parents
events.summary: Esau marries foreign women, causing significant distress to Isaac and Rebekah.
developments: At age forty, he marries Judith and Basemath, both Hittites (Genesis 26:34)
These marriages made life 'bitter' for his parents (Genesis 26:35)
The choice showed a disregard for the family's covenantal separation from Canaan (Genesis 26:34-35)
He later marries a daughter of Ishmael to try and please his father (Genesis 28:8-9)
events.significance: His marital choices further distanced him from the patriarchal promise.

events.title: The Stolen Blessing
events.summary: Jacob deceives Isaac to take the blessing intended for Esau, leading to a family crisis.
developments: Isaac intends to bless his favorite son Esau (Genesis 27:1-4)
Jacob disguises himself and receives the blessing of the firstborn (Genesis 27:18-29)
Esau returns and cries with an 'exceeding great and bitter cry' (Genesis 27:34)
Isaac gives Esau a secondary blessing regarding living by the sword (Genesis 27:39-40)
events.significance: The loss of the blessing finalized the transfer of the covenant line to Jacob.

events.title: Vow of Revenge
events.summary: Filled with hatred, Esau plans to murder Jacob after their father's death.
developments: Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing (Genesis 27:41)
He says in his heart, 'The days of mourning for my father are at hand; then I will kill my brother' (Genesis 27:41)
Rebekah hears of the threat and sends Jacob away to Haran (Genesis 27:42-45)
The threat of Esau’s violence drives Jacob into exile for twenty years (Genesis 28:1-5)
events.significance: Esau’s anger became the defining characteristic of the brothers' relationship for decades.

events.title: The Reunion
events.summary: After twenty years, Esau meets the returning Jacob and shows unexpected mercy.
developments: Jacob hears Esau is coming with 400 men and is terrified (Genesis 32:6-7)
Jacob sends massive waves of gifts to appease his brother (Genesis 32:13-21)
Esau runs to meet him, embraces him, and kisses him (Genesis 33:4)
Esau initially refuses the gifts, saying 'I have enough, my brother' (Genesis 33:9)
events.significance: The reconciliation demonstrated a softening of Esau's heart and the resolution of the family feud.

events.title: Father of the Edomites
events.summary: Esau settles in Mount Seir and becomes the progenitor of a major nation.
developments: He moves his household away from Jacob because their possessions were too great (Genesis 36:6-7)
He settles in the hill country of Seir (Genesis 36:8)
Esau is explicitly identified as 'Edom' (Genesis 36:1)
The chapter records the long list of chiefs and kings descended from him (Genesis 36:15-43)
events.significance: Esau established a powerful, secular kingdom that would later become Israel's rival.

events.title: The Example of Election
events.summary: The biblical authors use Esau to illustrate God’s sovereign choice in salvation history.
developments: God says through Malachi, 'I have loved Jacob but Esau I have hated' (Malachi 1:2-3)
Paul quotes this to show God’s choice is not based on human works (Romans 9:10-13)
The choice was made before the twins were born or had done anything good or bad (Romans 9:11)
Esau represents the rejection of the promise through divine sovereignty (Romans 9:13)
events.significance: Esau became a theological cornerstone for the doctrine of election.

events.title: The Profane Man
events.summary: Hebrews warns the church not to be like Esau, who traded his inheritance for a meal.
developments: He is called a 'profane' or 'unholy' person (Hebrews 12:16)
The text notes that afterward, he desired to inherit the blessing but was rejected (Hebrews 12:17)
He found no chance to repent, though he sought it with tears (Hebrews 12:17)
His life is used as a warning against apostasy and the love of the world (Hebrews 12:15-17)
events.significance: Esau remains the definitive biblical warning against short-term thinking and spiritual neglect.

label: Esther
overview: Esther, born Hadassah, was a young Jewish orphan in Susa who rose to become the Queen of the Persian Empire during the reign of Ahasuerus (Xerxes I). Under the guidance of her cousin Mordecai, she initially kept her Jewish identity a secret while winning the king’s favor. When Haman orchestrated a genocidal plot against her people, Esther demonstrated extraordinary courage by risking her life to approach the king unsummoned. Through a series of strategic banquets and a desperate plea, she exposed Haman’s malice and secured a decree that allowed the Jews to defend themselves. Her story is the foundation for the Feast of Purim and serves as a primary example of God’s providential care for His people through 'such a time as this.'

events.title: Orphan and Exile
events.summary: Esther is raised by her cousin Mordecai in Susa after the death of her parents.
developments: She is identified as Hadassah, the daughter of Abihail (Esther 2:7, 15)
Mordecai took her as his own daughter when her parents died (Esther 2:7)
She is described as beautiful and lovely to behold (Esther 2:7)
She is among the young women taken to the king's harem after Vashti's removal (Esther 2:8)
events.significance: Her humble and tragic beginnings highlight the scale of her future elevation.

events.title: Favor in the Harem
events.summary: Esther wins the respect of the palace officials and remains loyal to Mordecai's instructions.
developments: Hegai, the eunuch in charge, is pleased with her and gives her special treatment (Esther 2:9)
She does not reveal her people or kindred as Mordecai commanded (Esther 2:10)
She asks for nothing beyond what Hegai advised (Esther 2:15)
She wins favor in the sight of all who saw her (Esther 2:15)
events.significance: Her wisdom and restraint distinguished her from the other candidates.

events.title: Crowned as Queen
events.summary: King Ahasuerus selects Esther as his new queen, and a great festival is held in her honor.
developments: The king loved Esther more than all the other women (Esther 2:17)
He sets the royal crown on her head (Esther 2:17)
He makes her queen instead of Vashti in the seventh year of his reign (Esther 2:16-17)
The king gives a great feast called 'Esther's Feast' (Esther 2:18)
events.significance: This was the providential positioning of a believer at the heart of secular power.

events.title: The Challenge of Mordecai
events.summary: When Haman's plot is revealed, Esther is challenged to act on behalf of her people.
developments: Mordecai informs Esther of the decree to destroy the Jews (Esther 4:7-8)
Esther initially expresses fear because of the law regarding unsummoned visits to the king (Esther 4:11)
Mordecai warns her that she will not escape just because she is in the palace (Esther 4:13)
He asks the famous question, 'Who knows whether you have come to the kingdom for such a time as this?' (Esther 4:14)
events.significance: This moment defined Esther’s mission and required a move from safety to sacrifice.

events.title: Decision and Fasting
events.summary: Esther commits to the mission, calling for a national fast to seek divine favor.
developments: She instructs Mordecai to gather all the Jews in Susa for a three-day fast (Esther 4:16)
She and her young women fast in the same way (Esther 4:16)
She declares her famous resolution: 'If I perish, I perish' (Esther 4:16)
This marked her total dependence on God rather than her own beauty (Esther 4:16)
events.significance: Esther transitioned from a passive recipient of fate to a proactive leader.

events.title: Approaching the King
events.summary: Esther enters the inner court unsummoned, risking execution for the sake of her people.
developments: She puts on her royal robes and stands in the inner court (Esther 5:1)
The king sees her and extends his golden scepter (Esther 5:2)
He offers her up to half his kingdom (Esther 5:3)
She strategically invites the king and Haman to a private banquet (Esther 5:4)
events.significance: Her courage was met with immediate royal favor, opening the door for her plan.

events.title: Exposing the Enemy
events.summary: At the second banquet, Esther reveals her identity and identifies Haman as the villain.
developments: The king asks again for her petition (Esther 7:2)
She begs for her life and the lives of her people (Esther 7:3-4)
She names Haman as 'this wicked Haman,' the foe and enemy (Esther 7:6)
The king is enraged and orders Haman to be hanged (Esther 7:7-10)
events.significance: Esther’s transparency and timing led to the sudden downfall of the Jewish people's greatest enemy.

events.title: The Counter-Decree
events.summary: Esther pleads for a second decree to save the Jews from the irreversible law of Haman.
developments: She falls at the king's feet and weeps for her people (Esther 8:3)
The king gives her and Mordecai authority to write a new edict (Esther 8:8)
The new law allows the Jews to arm and defend themselves on the appointed day (Esther 8:11)
She and Mordecai send the news to all 127 provinces (Esther 8:9-14)
events.significance: She secured the legal means for her nation’s survival.

events.title: Victory and Institution
events.summary: The Jews defeat their enemies, and Esther ensures the event is remembered forever.
developments: The Jews strike down all their enemies throughout the empire (Esther 9:5)
Esther requests a second day of defense for Susa (Esther 9:13)
She and Mordecai write letters to establish the Feast of Purim (Esther 9:29)
She remains a powerful figure in the Persian court alongside Mordecai (Esther 10:3)
events.significance: Esther’s legacy became a permanent part of the Jewish liturgical and national identity.

label: Euodia
overview: Euodia was a prominent woman in the church of Philippi who had served alongside the Apostle Paul in the early stages of his ministry. She is mentioned by name in Paul’s letter to the Philippians because of a public disagreement between her and another leader, Syntyche. While the specific nature of their conflict is not recorded, Paul describes them both as women who 'labored side by side' with him in the cause of the gospel. He pleaded with them to live in harmony and asked a fellow worker to assist them in their reconciliation. Euodia represents the reality of interpersonal conflict among even the most dedicated and faithful leaders in the church.

events.title: Fellow Laborer with Paul
events.summary: Euodia is recognized for her active and courageous role in the initial mission to Philippi.
developments: Paul writes to the Philippians from a Roman prison (Philippians 1:12-13)
He notes that Euodia labored 'side by side' with him in the gospel (Philippians 4:3)
Her work was part of the foundational effort to establish the first church in Europe (Philippians 4:3)
She is grouped with Clement and other fellow workers of the apostle (Philippians 4:3)
events.significance: Euodia was a high-level partner in ministry, not just a casual observer.

events.title: The Public Conflict
events.summary: Paul addresses a rift between Euodia and Syntyche that threatened the peace of the congregation.
developments: Paul mentions Euodia by name to emphasize the urgency of the issue (Philippians 4:2)
He entreats her directly to 'agree in the Lord' (Philippians 4:2)
The naming in the letter suggests the disagreement was well-known and significant (Philippians 4:2)
The command to agree 'in the Lord' indicates the solution was spiritual rather than merely social (Philippians 4:2)
events.significance: Her story serves as a reminder that personal friction can occur even among 'fellow workers'.

events.title: Assistance in Reconciliation
events.summary: Paul asks a trusted 'true companion' to intervene and help the two women resolve their dispute.
developments: Paul appeals to an unnamed 'true companion' (syzygos) (Philippians 4:3)
He explicitly says, 'Help these women' (Philippians 4:3)
He reminds the helper of the women’s past faithfulness and sacrifice (Philippians 4:3)
The goal was to restore the unity of the church's leadership team (Philippians 4:1-3)
events.significance: This highlighted the community's responsibility in facilitating peace between conflicted members.

events.title: Written in the Book of Life
events.summary: Despite her current conflict, Paul affirms Euodia’s eternal standing and security.
developments: Paul notes that those involved in the work have their names in the Book of Life (Philippians 4:3)
This includes Euodia and the others who labored with him (Philippians 4:3)
The affirmation ensures that her character was defined by her faith rather than her flaw (Philippians 4:3)
The letter ends with a call for joy and peace for all the Philippians (Philippians 4:4-9)
events.significance: Euodia’s legacy is ultimately one of salvation and service rather than division.

label: Eutychus
overview: Eutychus was a young man in Troas who became the subject of a dramatic miracle during the Apostle Paul's final journey to Jerusalem. While listening to Paul’s exceptionally long sermon in a crowded upper room, Eutychus fell asleep and tumbled out of a third-story window to his death. Paul immediately went down, threw himself upon the youth, and declared that he was alive. The resurrection of Eutychus brought great comfort to the local believers and allowed Paul to continue his farewell teaching until daybreak. His story is a vivid example of the apostolic power present in the early church and provides a humanizing glimpse into the intensive teaching sessions of the New Testament era.

events.title: The Late Night Sermon
events.summary: Eutychus is among a group of believers in Troas gathering to hear Paul’s final instructions.
developments: They are gathered in an upper room on the first day of the week (Acts 20:7)
Paul intends to depart the next day and prolongs his speech until midnight (Acts 20:7)
There were many lamps in the upper room, possibly contributing to the heat and stuffiness (Acts 20:8)
Eutychus is sitting on the window sill as Paul talks on and on (Acts 20:9)
events.significance: The setting provided the conditions for a natural accident during a spiritual meeting.

events.title: The Fatal Fall
events.summary: Overcome by sleep, the young man falls three stories to the ground.
developments: Eutychus sinks into a deep sleep as Paul continues to preach (Acts 20:9)
Being overcome by sleep, he falls down from the third story (Acts 20:9)
He is picked up dead by those who rushed down to help (Acts 20:9)
The death caused an immediate crisis in the assembly (Acts 20:9-10)
events.significance: The tragedy threatened to turn a night of teaching into a night of mourning.

events.title: Miraculous Restoration
events.summary: Paul performs a restorative miracle, bringing Eutychus back to life through physical contact.
developments: Paul goes down and bends over him, taking him in his arms (Acts 20:10)
Paul tells the crowd, 'Do not be alarmed, for his life is in him' (Acts 20:10)
The physical action mirrors the miracles of Elijah and Elisha (Acts 20:10)
The young man is revived and restored to the community (Acts 20:12)
events.significance: This event demonstrated that Paul possessed the full authority and power of an apostle.

events.title: Aftermath and Comfort
events.summary: Following the miracle, the group returns to the room to celebrate and continue their fellowship.
developments: Paul goes back up, breaks bread, and eats (Acts 20:11)
He continues to converse with them until daybreak (Acts 20:11)
They take the youth away alive and are 'not a little comforted' (Acts 20:12)
The miracle solidified the believers' faith before Paul's final departure (Acts 20:12-13)
events.significance: Eutychus’s life became a living proof of the gospel's power over death.

label: Ezekiel
overview: Ezekiel was a priest and a prophet who was taken into Babylonian captivity during the second wave of exiles. Called by God near the Chebar canal, his ministry was defined by elaborate, often bizarre symbolic actions and grand, apocalyptic visions, such as the 'wheels within wheels' and the valley of dry bones. Ezekiel acted as a watchman for the house of Israel, delivering messages of stern judgment for their past idolatry but also profound hope for a future spiritual restoration. His work emphasized the individual responsibility of the soul before God and the ultimate return of the divine glory to a new, idealized Temple.

events.title: The Vision of Divine Glory
events.summary: While among the exiles in Babylon, Ezekiel receives a spectacular vision of God’s mobile throne.
developments: He is identified as the son of Buzi, a priest (Ezekiel 1:3)
The vision occurs in the fifth year of King Jehoiachin's exile (Ezekiel 1:2)
He sees four living creatures and wheels within wheels (Ezekiel 1:5-21)
He witnesses the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the Lord (Ezekiel 1:28)
He falls on his face in awe of the revelation (Ezekiel 1:28)
events.significance: This established that God's presence was not limited to the Temple in Jerusalem.

events.title: Commission as a Watchman
events.summary: God calls Ezekiel to speak to a 'rebellious house' and eat a scroll containing his message.
developments: The Spirit enters him and sets him on his feet (Ezekiel 2:2)
He is told to eat a scroll filled with lamentation and woe (Ezekiel 3:1-3)
The scroll tastes as sweet as honey in his mouth (Ezekiel 3:3)
God makes his face as hard as flint to withstand the people's rejection (Ezekiel 3:8-9)
He is appointed as a 'watchman' for the house of Israel (Ezekiel 3:17)
events.significance: Ezekiel carried the heavy burden of warning a people who would likely not listen.

events.title: The Symbolic Siege
events.summary: Ezekiel performs series of dramatic street-theater acts to illustrate the coming fall of Jerusalem.
developments: He lies on his side for 390 days to bear the punishment of Israel (Ezekiel 4:4-5)
He cooks his food over cow dung as a sign of the coming famine (Ezekiel 4:12-15)
He shaves his head and beard with a sword, dividing the hair into thirds (Ezekiel 5:1-2)
The hair represents the different fates of Jerusalem's inhabitants (Ezekiel 5:12)
events.significance: His very life and body became a living prophetic message to the exiles.

events.title: The Departure of Glory
events.summary: In a vision, Ezekiel is transported to Jerusalem to see the abominations in the Temple.
developments: He sees the elders of Israel worshipping creeping things in secret (Ezekiel 8:10-12)
He witnesses women weeping for the god Tammuz at the gate (Ezekiel 8:14)
He watches as the glory of the Lord lifts from the threshold of the Temple (Ezekiel 10:18)
The glory moves to the mountain east of the city (Mount of Olives) (Ezekiel 11:23)
events.significance: The departure of the glory signaled that the Temple was no longer protected from destruction.

events.title: The Death of His Wife
events.summary: Ezekiel’s personal tragedy is used by God as a final sign to the exiles.
developments: The Lord warns him He will take 'the delight of your eyes' with a blow (Ezekiel 24:16)
His wife dies in the evening, and he is forbidden from mourning publicly (Ezekiel 24:18)
His lack of mourning represents how the people will react when the Temple falls (Ezekiel 24:21-24)
Word arrives that Jerusalem has been captured (Ezekiel 33:21)
events.significance: This event marked the transition of his ministry from judgment to future hope.

events.title: The Shepherd Prophecy
