The land had rest for forty years following his downfall (Judges 5:31)
events.significance: Jabin's destruction marked the final time a Canaanite king would rule over Israel.

label: Jacob
overview: Jacob, the son of Isaac and Rebekah, was the third of the great patriarchs and the father of the twelve tribes of Israel. His life was a complex journey of struggle, beginning with his birth as a twin who 'grasped the heel' of his brother Esau. Known for his cleverness and occasionally his deception, Jacob eventually experienced a series of transformative encounters with God, most notably his vision of a ladder reaching to heaven and his all-night wrestling match with a divine messenger. Through these trials, he was renamed Israel (one who strives with God), and his focus shifted from self-reliance to spiritual legacy. Jacob’s later years were defined by the drama of his son Joseph in Egypt, concluding with his prophetic blessings over his sons and the settling of his family in Goshen.

events.title: Birth and the Red Stew
events.summary: Jacob is born as a twin, grabbing his brother’s heel, and later buys Esau's birthright.
developments: He comes out holding Esau's heel (Genesis 25:26)
He is described as a 'quiet man, dwelling in tents' favored by Rebekah (Genesis 25:27-28)
He convinces a famished Esau to trade his birthright for stew (Genesis 25:31-33)
The transaction reveals Jacob’s early opportunism (Genesis 25:34)
events.significance: Jacob secured the legal rights of the firstborn through shrewd negotiation.

events.title: The Stolen Blessing
events.summary: Jacob deceives his blind father Isaac to obtain the primary patriarchal blessing.
developments: He disguises himself with goatskins to feel like hairy Esau (Genesis 27:16)
He lies directly to Isaac to receive the blessing of fertility and rule (Genesis 27:19-29)
Esau vows to kill him after Isaac's death (Genesis 27:41)
Jacob is forced to flee to his uncle Laban in Haran (Genesis 27:42-28:5)
events.significance: The theft of the blessing caused a twenty-year family exile and a lifelong feud.

events.title: Vision at Bethel
events.summary: During his flight, Jacob dreams of a ladder reaching to heaven and receives a divine promise.
developments: He sees angels of God ascending and descending on a ladder (Genesis 28:12)
The Lord confirms the land and offspring promise of Abraham to him (Genesis 28:13-15)
Jacob awakes and declares the place 'the gate of heaven' (Genesis 28:17)
He names the place Bethel and vows to serve God if He protects him (Genesis 28:18-22)
events.significance: This was Jacob's first personal, direct encounter with the God of his fathers.

events.title: Life with Laban
events.summary: Jacob arrives in Paddan-aram and meets his match in the deceptive Laban.
developments: He falls in love with Rachel and agrees to work seven years for her (Genesis 29:18-20)
Laban deceives him into marrying Leah first (Genesis 29:23-25)
Jacob works another seven years for Rachel (Genesis 29:27-30)
He spends a total of twenty years serving Laban (Genesis 31:41)
events.significance: His years in Haran were a period of testing and family formation.

events.title: The Twelve Sons
events.summary: During his years in Haran, Jacob fathers twelve sons through his two wives and two servants.
developments: Leah bears Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah (Genesis 29:32-35)
Bilhah and Zilpah bear four sons to Jacob (Genesis 30:4-13)
Leah bears Issachar, Zebulun, and Dinah (Genesis 30:17-21)
Rachel finally gives birth to Joseph (Genesis 30:22-24)
events.significance: This period saw the literal birth of the tribes of the nation of Israel.

events.title: Flight from Haran
events.summary: Following God's command, Jacob secretly leaves Laban to return to Canaan.
developments: God tells Jacob to return to the land of his kindred (Genesis 31:3)
Laban pursues him but is warned by God in a dream (Genesis 31:24)
They make a covenant of peace at a heap of stones (Genesis 31:44-53)
Jacob departs from his father-in-law forever (Genesis 31:55)
events.significance: Jacob regained his independence and began his journey toward reconciliation.

events.title: Wrestling at Peniel
events.summary: Jacob wrestles with a man until daybreak and receives a new name and a blessing.
developments: He sends his family across the Jabbok and remains alone (Genesis 32:22-24)
A man wrestles with him, touching his hip socket (Genesis 32:24-25)
Jacob refuses to let go until he is blessed (Genesis 32:26)
His name is changed to Israel ('He strives with God') (Genesis 32:28)
events.significance: The new name symbolized a fundamental shift in Jacob's identity and spiritual status.

events.title: Reconciliation with Esau
events.summary: Jacob meets his brother with fear, but is greeted with unexpected brotherly love.
developments: Jacob bows seven times as he approaches (Genesis 33:3)
Esau runs to meet him, embraces him, and kisses him (Genesis 33:4)
They weep together and introduce their families (Genesis 33:4-7)
Jacob settles for a time near Shechem (Genesis 33:18)
events.significance: The resolution of the family feud allowed Jacob to dwell in the land in peace.

events.title: Spiritual Cleansing and Grief
events.summary: Jacob returns to Bethel to fulfill his vow and suffers the loss of Rachel.
developments: He commands his household to put away foreign gods (Genesis 35:2)
He builds an altar at Bethel as God commanded (Genesis 35:7)
Rachel dies giving birth to Benjamin near Bethlehem (Genesis 35:16-19)
Jacob eventually buries his father Isaac in Hebron (Genesis 35:27-29)
events.significance: This was a period of both spiritual renewal and intense personal sorrow.

events.title: The Loss and Recovery of Joseph
events.summary: Jacob mourns for many years after his sons report that Joseph is dead.
developments: His sons bring him Joseph's blood-stained coat (Genesis 37:31-33)
Jacob refuses to be comforted and mourns for decades (Genesis 37:34-35)
A famine forces his sons to go to Egypt (Genesis 42:1-5)
Jacob is revitalized when he learns Joseph is alive and ruler of Egypt (Genesis 45:26-28)
events.significance: The family drama led to the migration of the entire nation into Egypt.

events.title: Life in Egypt and Final Blessings
events.summary: Jacob spends his final seventeen years in Goshen and blesses his sons before his death.
developments: He is presented to Pharaoh and blesses the king (Genesis 47:7-10)
He adopts and blesses Joseph’s sons, Ephraim and Manasseh (Genesis 48:1-20)
He delivers a lengthy poetic prophecy concerning each of his twelve sons (Genesis 49:1-27)
He gives specific instructions for his burial in Canaan (Genesis 49:29-32)
events.significance: Jacob’s final words established the future tribal identities of the nation.

events.title: Death and Funeral
events.summary: Jacob dies at age 147 and is given a royal Egyptian funeral procession back to Canaan.
developments: He is embalmed in the Egyptian manner (Genesis 50:2-3)
A great company of chariots and horsemen accompanies the body (Genesis 50:9)
His sons bury him in the cave of Machpelah as he requested (Genesis 50:13)
The Egyptians mourn for seventy days (Genesis 50:3)
events.significance: His burial in Canaan was a statement of faith that his people would eventually return to the land.

label: Jael
overview: Jael was the wife of Heber the Kenite who became the unexpected heroine of the war against King Jabin of Canaan. During the rout of the Canaanite army, the general Sisera sought refuge in her tent, believing her household was a neutral ally. Jael welcomed him with deceptive hospitality, providing him milk and a place to rest. Once he was asleep, she performed a bold and gruesome act of execution, driving a tent peg through his temple. Jael’s action fulfilled Deborah’s prophecy that the glory of the victory would go to a woman and earned her the title 'most blessed of women' in the victory song of Israel.

events.title: The Strategic Refuge
events.summary: Jael encounters the fleeing Canaanite general Sisera at her tent in the wilderness.
developments: Sisera flees on foot after the defeat at Mount Tabor (Judges 4:17)
He goes to the tent of Jael because there was peace between Jabin and the Kenites (Judges 4:17)
Jael goes out to meet him and says, 'Turn aside, my lord' (Judges 4:18)
She covers him with a rug or mantle for warmth and concealment (Judges 4:18)
events.significance: Her invitation was a calculated act of deception against the enemy of Israel.

events.title: Hospitality and Deception
events.summary: Jael provides for Sisera’s physical needs to lower his guard.
developments: Sisera asks for a little water because he is thirsty (Judges 4:19)
Jael opens a skin of milk and gives him a drink (Judges 4:19)
Sisera instructs her to stand at the door and lie if anyone asks (Judges 4:20)
He falls into a deep, exhausted sleep (Judges 4:21)
events.significance: The 'milk instead of water' was a sign of extreme, lulling hospitality.

events.title: The Execution
events.summary: Jael uses her domestic tools to perform a lethal and decisive military act.
developments: She takes a tent peg and a hammer in her hand (Judges 4:21)
She approaches him softly while he is fast asleep (Judges 4:21)
Jael drives the peg into his temple so it goes into the ground (Judges 4:21)
Sisera dies instantly at her hand (Judges 4:21)
events.significance: Her unconventional method fulfilled the prophecy of Deborah regarding Sisera's fate.

events.title: Presentation to Barak
events.summary: Jael reveals the dead general to the pursuing Israelite commander.
developments: Barak arrives at her tent in pursuit of Sisera (Judges 4:22)
Jael meets him and says, 'Come, and I will show you the man you are seeking' (Judges 4:22)
Barak enters and sees his enemy dead with the peg in his head (Judges 4:22)
This moment finalized the Israelite victory over the Canaanite forces (Judges 4:23)
events.significance: Jael proved that God can use the most unlikely people and places to accomplish His will.

events.title: Commemoration in Song
events.summary: Jael is celebrated as a national hero in the victory hymn of Deborah and Barak.
developments: The song declares, 'Most blessed of women be Jael' (Judges 5:24)
It recounts the specific details of the milk and the hammer (Judges 5:25-26)
She is praised for her strength and her strike (Judges 5:26)
The lyrics contrast her action with the expectations of Sisera's mother (Judges 5:28-30)
events.significance: Her name was etched into the liturgical memory of the nation.

label: Jahaziel
overview: Jahaziel was a Levite of the sons of Asaph who received a powerful prophetic message during a time of national crisis for King Jehoshaphat. As a massive coalition of Moabites and Ammonites approached Jerusalem, Jahaziel was filled with the Spirit of the Lord in the midst of the congregation. He famously told the king and the people, 'Do not be afraid... for the battle is not yours, but God’s.' His prophecy provided specific military instructions that led to a miraculous victory without a physical fight. Jahaziel remains an example of how God uses the prophetic voice to provide strategic guidance and spiritual encouragement.

events.title: The Crisis of the Invasion
events.summary: Jahaziel is present in the Temple as King Jehoshaphat leads the nation in prayer.
developments: A great multitude from Edom and Moab approaches Jerusalem (2 Chronicles 20:1-2)
The whole nation of Judah gathers to seek help from the Lord (2 Chronicles 20:3-4)
Jehosaphat delivers a passionate prayer in the new court (2 Chronicles 20:5-12)
The entire assembly stands before the Lord with their children (2 Chronicles 20:13)
events.significance: The atmosphere was one of total national dependence on divine intervention.

events.title: The Spirit's Outpouring
events.summary: The Spirit of the Lord comes upon Jahaziel in the middle of the assembly.
developments: He is identified as the son of Zechariah, a Levite of the sons of Asaph (2 Chronicles 20:14)
The prophecy arrives spontaneously during the prayer meeting (2 Chronicles 20:14)
He calls for the attention of all Judah and the king (2 Chronicles 20:15)
He speaks with immediate divine authority: 'Thus says the Lord to you' (2 Chronicles 20:15)
events.significance: Jahaziel acted as the mouthpiece for God's immediate response to the king's prayer.

events.title: The Word of Encouragement
events.summary: Jahaziel delivers the foundational truth that God will fight on Israel’s behalf.
developments: He tells them, 'Do not be afraid and do not be dismayed' (2 Chronicles 20:15)
He declares, 'the battle is not yours but God's' (2 Chronicles 20:15)
He tells them they will not even need to fight in this battle (2 Chronicles 20:17)
He commands them to 'stand firm, hold your position' (2 Chronicles 20:17)
events.significance: The prophecy redefined the nature of the coming conflict as a spiritual event.

events.title: The Tactical Instructions
events.summary: Jahaziel provides the specific location where the enemy will be encountered.
developments: He tells them to go down against the enemy 'tomorrow' (2 Chronicles 20:16)
He says the coalition will be coming up by the ascent of Ziz (2 Chronicles 20:16)
He identifies the exact spot: at the end of the valley, east of the wilderness of Jeruel (2 Chronicles 20:16)
The people respond by falling down and worshipping (2 Chronicles 20:18)
events.significance: His prophetic accuracy gave the military leaders the confidence to move.

events.title: Fulfillment and Victory
events.summary: Israel follows the prophet’s word and witnesses the total destruction of the enemy by God.
developments: Jehosaphat appoints singers to lead the army, praising the Lord (2 Chronicles 20:21)
The Lord sets ambushes, causing the coalition to destroy each other (2 Chronicles 20:22-23)
Judah finds only dead bodies and a massive amount of spoil (2 Chronicles 20:24-25)
The valley is renamed 'the Valley of Beracah' (Blessing) (2 Chronicles 20:26)
events.significance: The victory was the physical validation of Jahaziel's prophetic message.

label: Jair
overview: Jair was a prominent leader and judge of Israel from the region of Gilead who led the nation for twenty-two years. He is primarily known for his thirty sons, who were influential enough to ride on thirty donkeys and possess thirty distinct cities in Gilead, known as Havvoth-jair. Jair’s judgeship followed that of Tola and occurred during a period of relative expansion and tribal stability in the Transjordan. He was eventually buried in the city of Kamon, leaving a legacy of family prosperity and territorial development. The name Jair was also held by an earlier descendant of Manasseh who captured several villages during the initial conquest.

events.title: Conquest of Gilead
events.summary: An early Jair, a descendant of Manasseh, captures regional villages during the settlement.
developments: Jair the son of Manasseh goes and captures several villages (Numbers 32:41)
He names them Havvoth-jair ('Villages of Jair') (Numbers 32:41)
He receives the region of Argob as an inheritance (Deuteronomy 3:14)
This established the family name as a dominant force in the Transjordan (Deuteronomy 3:1-14)
events.significance: The early Jair was a pioneer of Israelite settlement east of the Jordan.

events.title: Succession to the Judgeship
events.summary: A later Jair arises from Gilead to judge the entire nation of Israel.
developments: He arises after the death of the judge Tola (Judges 10:3)
He is identified as Jair the Gileadite (Judges 10:3)
He leads the nation for a total of twenty-two years (Judges 10:3)
His rule contributed to the stability of the pre-monarchy period (Judges 10:1-3)
events.significance: His rise showed the continued leadership influence of the eastern tribes.

events.title: Dynastic Prosperity
events.summary: The text highlights the status of Jair’s large and influential family.
developments: He had thirty sons who rode on thirty donkeys (Judges 10:4)
The donkeys were a mark of high social standing and peace (Judges 10:4)
They possessed thirty cities in the land of Gilead (Judges 10:4)
The cities were still called Havvoth-jair 'to this day' (Judges 10:4)
events.significance: Jair’s tenure was a period of material and political growth for his clan.

events.title: Death and Burial
events.summary: Jair dies after his long tenure and is buried in his home region.
developments: Jair died after his twenty-two year judgeship (Judges 10:5)
He was buried in Kamon (Judges 10:5)
Following his death, Israel again did evil and served the Baals (Judges 10:6)
His death marked the end of a long season of rest for the nation (Judges 10:3-6)
events.significance: His burial signaled a transition into a new era of spiritual testing.

events.title: Genealogical Record
events.summary: The lineage of Jair is preserved to explain the family’s prominence in Judah and Manasseh.
developments: He is the son of Segub and grandson of Hezron (1 Chronicles 2:21-22)
His father married a daughter of Machir the father of Gilead (1 Chronicles 2:21)
This union connected the tribe of Judah to the half-tribe of Manasseh (1 Chronicles 2:21-22)
Jair had twenty-three cities in the land of Gilead at that time (1 Chronicles 2:22)
events.significance: The records validate the legal and tribal claims of Jair's descendants.

label: Jairus
overview: Jairus was a ruler of the synagogue, likely in Capernaum, who became the subject of one of Jesus' most profound miracles. When his twelve-year-old daughter was at the point of death, Jairus humbled himself by falling at Jesus’ feet and begging for help. Despite a delay on the road and a report that the child had already died, Jesus encouraged Jairus to 'only believe.' In a dramatic display of authority over death, Jesus entered Jairus’ house and raised the girl to life in the presence of her parents. Jairus’ story serves as a testament to the power of desperate faith and Jesus' compassion for those in deep personal grief.

events.title: The Desperate Petition
events.summary: Jairus approaches Jesus with an urgent request for his dying daughter.
developments: He is identified as Jairus, a ruler of the synagogue (Mark 5:22)
He falls at Jesus' feet, a sign of extreme humility for an official (Mark 5:22)
He implores Jesus, saying his little daughter is at the point of death (Mark 5:23)
He asks Jesus to lay His hands on her so she may be made well (Mark 5:23)
events.significance: This act showed that Jesus' authority was recognized even by some of the religious elite.

events.title: The Delay on the Road
events.summary: While heading to Jairus' house, Jesus stops to heal a woman with an issue of blood.
developments: A great crowd follows and throngs Jesus (Mark 5:24)
A woman touches Jesus' garment and is healed of her twelve-year affliction (Mark 5:25-30)
Jesus stops the entire procession to identify and speak with the woman (Mark 5:31-34)
Jairus must wait while the precious moments for his daughter slip away (Mark 5:24-34)
events.significance: This delay served as a severe test of Jairus’ patience and trust.

events.title: The Report of Death
events.summary: Messengers arrive from Jairus’ house with the news that it is too late for a miracle.
developments: They say, 'Your daughter is dead. Why trouble the Teacher any further?' (Mark 5:35)
Jesus overhears and tells Jairus, 'Do not fear, only believe' (Mark 5:36)
Jesus refuses to let anyone follow him except Peter, James, and John (Mark 5:37)
This moment shifted the goal from healing to resurrection (Mark 5:35-37)
events.significance: Jesus' words encouraged faith even in the face of total finality.

events.title: Confronting the Mourners
events.summary: Upon arrival, Jesus rebukes the professional mourners for their lack of faith.
developments: He sees a commotion and people weeping and wailing (Mark 5:38)
He asks why they are making a commotion and says the child is only sleeping (Mark 5:39)
The crowd laughs at Him in mockery (Mark 5:40)
Jesus puts them all outside and enters the room with the parents (Mark 5:40)
events.significance: The exclusion of the mockers emphasized the sanctity and privacy of the coming miracle.

events.title: The Raising of the Girl
events.summary: Jesus performs the resurrection and provides practical instructions for her care.
developments: He takes the child by the hand and says 'Talitha cumi' (Mark 5:41)
The girl, who was twelve years old, immediately gets up and walks (Mark 5:42)
The parents are overcome with great amazement (Mark 5:42)
Jesus tells them to give her something to eat (Mark 5:43)
events.significance: The miracle proved Jesus' absolute power over death and restored a family's hope.

label: Jambres
overview: Jambres, along with Jannes, is identified in the New Testament as one of the Egyptian magicians who opposed Moses in the court of Pharaoh. Although they are not named in the Book of Exodus, Jewish tradition and the Apostle Paul identify them as the sorcerers who attempted to mimic God’s miracles with their own enchantments. Paul uses Jambres as a negative example of those who 'oppose the truth,' characterizing them as men of depraved minds and counterfeit faith. Their ultimate failure in the face of God's power serves as a warning that those who resist the truth will eventually have their folly exposed to everyone.

events.title: Opposition in Pharaoh's Court
events.summary: Jambres (as one of the 'wise men') attempts to match the miracles performed by Moses.
developments: Pharaoh summons the wise men and sorcerers of Egypt (Exodus 7:11)
Jambres and his fellows throw down their staffs and they become serpents (Exodus 7:11-12)
Aaron's staff swallows their staffs, showing superior power (Exodus 7:12)
They attempt to keep Pharaoh's heart hardened against the Lord (Exodus 7:13)
events.significance: He represents the demonic counterfeit to divine authority.

events.title: The Limit of Sorcery
events.summary: The magicians successfully replicate the first two plagues but fail at the third.
developments: They turn water into blood using their secret arts (Exodus 7:22)
They bring frogs upon the land by their enchantments (Exodus 8:7)
They try but fail to produce gnats from the dust (Exodus 8:18)
They are forced to admit, 'This is the finger of God' (Exodus 8:19)
events.significance: The admission proved that their power was finite and subordinate to God.

events.title: Humiliation by Plagues
events.summary: The magicians are physically incapacitated by the very judgments they opposed.
developments: The plague of boils breaks out on all the Egyptians (Exodus 9:11)
The magicians cannot stand before Moses because of the boils (Exodus 9:11)
The boils affect the magicians and all the Egyptians alike (Exodus 9:11)
They disappear from the narrative following this public shaming (Exodus 9:11-12)
events.significance: The magicians’ inability to protect themselves exposed the vanity of their magic.

events.title: Named as False Teachers
events.summary: Paul uses the historical memory of Jambres to warn about the 'last days'.
developments: Paul explicitly names Jannes and Jambres (2 Timothy 3:8)
He says they 'opposed Moses' and so these men oppose the truth (2 Timothy 3:8)
He describes them as having depraved minds and being 'disqualified' regarding faith (2 Timothy 3:8)
Paul predicts they will not get very far, as their folly will be plain to all (2 Timothy 3:9)
events.significance: Jambres became a permanent biblical type for the religious deceiver.

label: James
overview: James was the name of several central figures in the New Testament, most notably James the son of Zebedee and James the brother of Jesus. James the son of Zebedee was one of the twelve apostles and part of Jesus’ inner circle alongside his brother John, witnessing the Transfiguration and the agony in Gethsemane. He was the first apostle to be martyred, executed by King Herod Agrippa I. James the brother of the Lord, though initially a skeptic, became the primary leader of the Jerusalem church after the resurrection and the author of the Epistle of James. Together, these figures represent the range of Christian service, from sacrificial martyrdom to the foundational governance and teaching of the global church.

events.title: The Call of the Son of Zebedee
events.summary: James leaves his fishing business to become one of the first disciples of Jesus.
developments: Jesus finds James and John in a boat with their father (Matthew 4:21)
They are mending their nets when Jesus calls them (Matthew 4:21)
They immediately leave the boat and their father to follow Him (Matthew 4:22)
Jesus gives them the nickname 'Boanerges' or 'Sons of Thunder' (Mark 3:17)
events.significance: This prompt obedience marked the beginning of James's foundational role in the apostolate.

events.title: Member of the Inner Circle
events.summary: James is selected by Jesus to witness the most private and powerful moments of His ministry.
developments: He is present for the raising of Jairus' daughter (Mark 5:37)
He witnesses the Transfiguration of Jesus on the high mountain (Matthew 17:1)
He is one of three taken deeper into Gethsemane during Jesus' agony (Mark 14:33)
This grouping shows James was one of the top three leaders among the Twelve (Mark 9:2)
events.significance: James had a level of direct revelation shared by almost no one else.

events.title: Ambition and the Cup
events.summary: James and John request positions of high status in Jesus' coming kingdom.
developments: They (or their mother) ask to sit at Jesus' right and left hands in glory (Matthew 20:20-21)
Jesus asks if they can drink the cup He is to drink (Matthew 20:22)
James and John confidently declare, 'We are able' (Matthew 20:22)
Jesus prophesies that they will indeed drink His cup of suffering (Matthew 20:23)
events.significance: This interaction transformed James's worldly ambition into a calling for sacrifice.

events.title: The First Apostolic Martyr
events.summary: James the son of Zebedee is executed by King Herod Agrippa I.
developments: Herod begins a violent persecution of the church (Acts 12:1)
He kills James the brother of John with the sword (Acts 12:2)
The death pleased the Jewish leadership (Acts 12:3)
This fulfilled Jesus’ prophecy that James would drink His cup (Acts 12:2)
events.significance: James was the first of the Twelve to seal his testimony with his blood.

events.title: James the Brother of Jesus
events.summary: The brother of Jesus is introduced as a family member and initially a skeptic.
developments: He is listed as the first of Jesus' brothers in Nazareth (Matthew 13:55)
The brothers are recorded as not believing in Him initially (John 7:5)
They attempt to restrain Jesus during His ministry (Mark 3:21, 31)
This James was separate from the apostles during the Galilean years (John 7:3-5)
events.significance: His journey from skeptic to believer is a major theme of the resurrection.

events.title: Resurrection Appearance
events.summary: Jesus appears specifically to His brother James after rising from the dead.
developments: Paul lists the witnesses of the risen Christ (1 Corinthians 15:5-6)
He notes that Jesus 'appeared to James, then to all the apostles' (1 Corinthians 15:7)
This personal encounter likely catalyzed the brothers' faith (1 Corinthians 15:7)
James is found with the apostles in the upper room after the ascension (Acts 1:14)
events.significance: The grace of Christ turned a doubting relative into a devoted servant.

events.title: Leader of the Jerusalem Church
events.summary: James, the brother of the Lord, emerges as the primary authority in the first church.
developments: Paul calls him one of the 'pillars' of the church along with Cephas and John (Galatians 2:9)
James presides over the Jerusalem Council regarding Gentiles (Acts 15:13)
He delivers the final verdict on the requirements for Gentile converts (Acts 15:19-21)
He is recognized as the head of the church when Paul visits Jerusalem (Acts 21:18)
events.significance: James provided the administrative and theological stability needed for the church's expansion.

events.title: Author of the Epistle
events.summary: James writes a letter to the scattered tribes of Israel emphasizing practical faith.
developments: He identifies himself as 'a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ' (James 1:1)
His letter focuses on the testing of faith and the need for works (James 1:2-4, 2:14-26)
He uses stern, wisdom-oriented language similar to the prophets (James 5:1-6)
The letter became a central part of the New Testament canon (James 1-5)
events.significance: James’s teaching ensured that the Gospel would be expressed through ethical living.

label: Jannes
overview: Jannes, paired with Jambres, is identified by the Apostle Paul as one of the Egyptian magicians who opposed Moses in Pharaoh's court. Although his name is not found in the Exodus account, it was well-preserved in Jewish oral tradition and cited by Paul to warn against the deceptive teachers of the 'last days.' Jannes used occult arts to replicate the early plagues, reinforcing Pharaoh's stubbornness and pride. His ultimate failure when he could not replicate the plague of gnats served as a definitive historical proof of the superiority of God’s power over human or demonic magic. Jannes remains a symbol of the intellectual and spiritual resistance that many face when presenting the truth of God.

events.title: Confrontation of Signs
events.summary: Jannes (as part of the Egyptian sorcerers) mimics the first sign given to Moses.
developments: Aaron throws down his staff and it becomes a serpent (Exodus 7:10)
Pharaoh summons the wise men and sorcerers (Exodus 7:11)
They perform the same feat by their secret arts (Exodus 7:11)
Aaron's staff swallows their staffs (Exodus 7:12)
events.significance: This established Jannes as the primary religious adversary to the Exodus.

events.title: Replicating the Plagues
events.summary: Jannes successfully matches the initial judgments, encouraging Pharaoh's rebellion.
developments: The magicians turn the water of the Nile into blood (Exodus 7:22)
They successfully bring up frogs upon the land (Exodus 8:7)
Their success convinces Pharaoh that Moses is merely a rival magician (Exodus 7:23)
This cooperation with evil delayed the release of the Israelites (Exodus 8:1-15)
events.significance: His actions illustrate how religious deception can mask divine judgment.

events.title: The Point of Failure
events.summary: Jannes and his companions reach the limit of their power during the third plague.
developments: Moses strikes the dust and it becomes gnats (Exodus 8:17)
The magicians try by their secret arts to produce gnats but they cannot (Exodus 8:18)
They tell Pharaoh, 'This is the finger of God' (Exodus 8:19)
Despite their admission, Pharaoh's heart remained hardened (Exodus 8:19)
events.significance: Jannes was the first to testify to the unique power of Yahweh in Egypt.

events.title: The Final Defeat
events.summary: The magicians are struck with boils and can no longer appear before Moses.
developments: A plague of boils breaks out on all the Egyptians (Exodus 9:11)
The magicians could not stand before Moses because of the boils (Exodus 9:11)
The text notes the boils were on the magicians specifically (Exodus 9:11)
They are silenced and removed from the narrative (Exodus 9:11)
events.significance: His physical suffering was a visible sign of his spiritual bankruptcy.

events.title: Prophetic Warning
events.summary: The Apostle Paul identifies Jannes by name as a type for those who oppose the gospel.
developments: Paul describes people who have the appearance of godliness but deny its power (2 Timothy 3:5)
He says 'Just as Jannes and Jambres opposed Moses,' so do these men (2 Timothy 3:8)
He calls them men of depraved minds (2 Timothy 3:8)
He promises their folly will be plain to all, as it was with Jannes (2 Timothy 3:9)
events.significance: Jannes remains the permanent biblical warning against using religious form to oppose God’s truth.

label: Jason
overview: Jason was a Christian believer in the city of Thessalonica who hosted the Apostle Paul and Silas during their second missionary journey. His hospitality turned into a legal crisis when a mob, incited by jealous religious leaders, attacked his home in search of the missionaries. Failing to find Paul, the mob dragged Jason before the city officials, accusing him of harboring men who 'turned the world upside down' and acted contrary to the decrees of Caesar. Jason was forced to post a security bond for the missionaries, an act of sacrificial loyalty that ensured their safety but required their immediate departure from the city. He is later mentioned by Paul in the letter to the Romans as a 'kinsman,' highlighting his enduring connection to the apostolic circle.

events.title: Hosting the Apostles
events.summary: Jason welcomes Paul and Silas into his home in Thessalonica to support their ministry.
developments: Paul preaches in the synagogue for three Sabbaths (Acts 17:2)
A great many devout Greeks and leading women join Paul (Acts 17:4)
Jason provides the domestic base for the fledgling church (Acts 17:7)
His hospitality makes him a target for the opposition (Acts 17:5)
events.significance: Jason's home became the first headquarters for the Gospel in Macedonia.

events.title: The Mob Attack
events.summary: Jealous Jews gather a crowd of 'wicked men' to assault Jason’s house.
developments: The mob attacks the house of Jason looking for the apostles (Acts 17:5)
They intend to bring the missionaries out to the crowd (Acts 17:5)
When they do not find them, they seize Jason instead (Acts 17:6)
They drag Jason and some brothers before the city authorities (Acts 17:6)
events.significance: Jason bore the brunt of the physical and legal persecution intended for Paul.

events.title: Accusation of Sedition
events.summary: Jason is charged with supporting rebels who proclaim another king, Jesus.
developments: The accusers claim Paul's team has 'turned the world upside down' (Acts 17:6)
They allege Jason has received them as guests in violation of Caesar's law (Acts 17:7)
The city authorities and the crowd are deeply disturbed by the charges (Acts 17:8)
Jason is held legally responsible for the actions of his guests (Acts 17:9)
events.significance: This was one of the first times the Gospel was framed as a political threat to Rome.

events.title: The Security Bond
events.summary: Jason pays a legal fine or bond to secure his release and the safety of the mission.
developments: The magistrates take money as security from Jason and the others (Acts 17:9)
This legal arrangement likely prevented Paul from returning soon (1 Thessalonians 2:18)
The brothers immediately send Paul and Silas away to Berea by night (Acts 17:10)
Jason remains in the city to lead the church under intense pressure (1 Thessalonians 1:6)
events.significance: His financial and legal sacrifice allowed the mission to continue westward.

events.title: Kinsman in Corinth
events.summary: Paul includes Jason in his greetings, acknowledging him as a relative and fellow-worker.
developments: Paul writes to the Romans from the city of Corinth (Romans 16:1)
Jason is listed among the senders of greetings (Romans 16:21)
Paul calls him a 'kinsman,' likely indicating shared Jewish heritage (Romans 16:21)
He is grouped with Lucius and Sosipater (Romans 16:21)
events.significance: Jason remained a faithful and mobile member of the Pauline network years later.

label: Jeconiah
overview: Jeconiah, also known as Jehoiachin or Coniah, was the nineteenth king of Judah and the last direct heir of the Davidic line to sit on the throne in Jerusalem. His reign lasted only three months and ten days before he was forced to surrender to Nebuchadnezzar during the first major siege of Jerusalem. Jeconiah was taken into Babylonian exile along with the Temple treasures and the nation's elite, spending thirty-seven years in prison. His life was marked by a severe divine curse through Jeremiah, stating that none of his offspring would prosper on the throne. However, his eventual release from prison and his inclusion in the genealogy of Jesus highlight the tension between divine judgment and the preservation of the Messianic hope.

events.title: Short and Evil Reign
events.summary: Jeconiah succeeds his father Jehoiakim at the age of eighteen during the Babylonian crisis.
developments: He becomes king in Jerusalem (2 Kings 24:8)
He reigns for only three months and ten days (2 Chronicles 36:9)
He does evil in the sight of the Lord as his father did (2 Kings 24:9)
Nebuchadnezzar's forces besiege the city during his reign (2 Kings 24:10)
events.significance: His reign marked the beginning of the end for the independent Kingdom of Judah.

events.title: Surrender and Deportation
events.summary: To prevent the city's total destruction, Jeconiah surrenders to the King of Babylon.
developments: He goes out to meet the King of Babylon with his mother and officials (2 Kings 24:12)
He is taken prisoner in the eighth year of Nebuchadnezzar (2 Kings 24:12)
Babylon carries away all the treasures of the house of the Lord (2 Kings 24:13)
Jeconiah is deported to Babylon with 10,000 captives (2 Kings 24:14-15)
events.significance: This was the first major 'Great Exile' of the Judean people.

events.title: The Curse of Coniah
events.summary: God pronounces a severe judgment on Jeconiah, removing him as a signet ring from His hand.
developments: God calls him Coniah and says he will be hurled into a foreign land (Jeremiah 22:24-27)
Jeremiah describes him as a 'despised, broken pot' (Jeremiah 22:28)
God commands: 'Write this man down as childless' (Jeremiah 22:30)
He is told no descendant will sit on the throne of David or rule in Judah (Jeremiah 22:30)
events.significance: The curse created a legal barrier for the Davidic succession.

events.title: Exaltation in Exile
events.summary: After thirty-seven years in prison, the new King of Babylon releases and honors Jeconiah.
developments: Evil-merodach becomes king of Babylon (2 Kings 25:27)
He speaks kindly to Jeconiah and brings him out of prison (2 Kings 25:28)
He gives Jeconiah a seat of honor above all other kings in Babylon (2 Kings 25:28)
Jeconiah eats at the king's table for the rest of his life (2 Kings 25:29-30)
events.significance: His restoration provided a glimmer of hope that God had not utterly abandoned the Davidic line.

events.title: Ancestry of Christ
events.summary: Despite the curse, Jeconiah is included in the genealogy of Jesus as the father of Shealtiel.
developments: Matthew lists Josiah as the father of Jeconiah (Matthew 1:11)
Jeconiah is the father of Shealtiel after the deportation (Matthew 1:12)
He is a direct link in the line leading to Joseph, the legal father of Jesus (Matthew 1:16)
His presence in the list shows God's mercy bypassing human limitations (Matthew 1:12-16)
events.significance: Jeconiah proves that God's plan for the Messiah was preserved through the Exile.

label: Jeduthun
overview: Jeduthun was a prominent Levite and one of the three principal musicians appointed by King David to oversee the music of the Tabernacle and the First Temple. He was the head of a major family guild of singers and musicians who 'prophesied with lyres, harps, and cymbals.' Known as 'the king’s seer,' Jeduthun played a vital role in national worship during the height of the United Monarchy and the subsequent reforms of Hezekiah and Josiah. His name appears in the superscriptions of several Psalms (39, 62, 77), indicating that he either composed these songs or was their primary performer. Jeduthun represents the elevation of artistic skill to a level of prophetic and liturgical significance in Israel.

events.title: Appointment at Gibeon
events.summary: David assigns Jeduthun and Heman to lead worship at the high place of Gibeon.
developments: They are stationed with the Tabernacle and the altar of burnt offering (1 Chronicles 16:39-41)
They are tasked with giving thanks to the Lord (1 Chronicles 16:41)
Jeduthun is responsible for the musical instruments and trumpets (1 Chronicles 16:42)
His sons are appointed as gatekeepers for the sanctuary (1 Chronicles 16:42)
events.significance: Jeduthun provided spiritual stability at the nation's primary sacrificial site.

events.title: The Prophetic Choir
events.summary: Jeduthun and his sons are set apart to 'prophesy' through their musical talents.
developments: David and the military chiefs select the sons of Jeduthun for service (1 Chronicles 25:1)
The group includes six sons under the direction of their father (1 Chronicles 25:3)
Jeduthun prophesies with the lyre in giving thanks and praise (1 Chronicles 25:3)
The work is organized into twenty-four divisions by lot (1 Chronicles 25:9-31)
events.significance: This established music as a legitimate form of prophetic revelation in Israel.

events.title: The Temple Dedication
events.summary: Jeduthun leads the massive musical celebration as the Ark enters Solomon’s Temple.
developments: He is listed among the chief singers dressed in fine linen (2 Chronicles 5:12)
He stands at the east side of the altar with Asaph and Heman (2 Chronicles 5:12)
The music is perfectly synchronized with 120 trumpeters (2 Chronicles 5:12-13)
The glory of the Lord fills the house as they praise God's steadfast love (2 Chronicles 5:13-14)
events.significance: Jeduthun was a key figure in the most magnificent worship event in biblical history.

events.title: Legacy of Reform
events.summary: Centuries later, the descendants of Jeduthun are central to the reforms of Hezekiah and Josiah.
developments: The 'sons of Jeduthun' assist in cleansing the Temple for Hezekiah (2 Chronicles 29:14)
They are restored to their assigned places in the choir (2 Chronicles 35:15)
Jeduthun is again called 'the king’s seer' in the record of Josiah (2 Chronicles 35:15)
The continuity of the guild ensured the preservation of Davidic worship (2 Chronicles 35:15)
events.significance: Jeduthun’s family line preserved the musical heartbeat of the nation for generations.

events.title: Post-Exilic Continuity
events.summary: Following the return from Babylon, the name of Jeduthun is used to validate the new Temple singers.
developments: Obadiah is identified as a descendant of Jeduthun (1 Chronicles 9:16)
The singers return to Jerusalem to resume their ancestral work (Nehemiah 11:17)
The genealogy traces them through generations to prove their legal right to lead (Nehemiah 11:17)
Jeduthun remains the standard for liturgical music in the Second Temple era (Nehemiah 11:17)
events.significance: His influence outlasted the destruction of the city and the Exile.

label: Jehoahaz
overview: Jehoahaz was the name of several kings, most notably the son of Jehu who reigned over Israel and the son of Josiah who briefly ruled Judah. Jehoahaz of Israel presided over a period of extreme national weakness, as the Aramean King Hazael decimated the Israelite army and oppressed the land. Despite his continued idolatry, Jehoahaz eventually sought the Lord, and God provided a 'deliverer' to relieve the Syrian pressure. Jehoahaz of Judah, also known as Shallum, reigned for only three months after his father’s death at Megiddo. He was deposed and taken into exile by Pharaoh Neco, becoming a symbol of the end of Judean independence.

events.title: Accession in the North
events.summary: Jehoahaz begins a seventeen-year reign over Israel during a time of intense foreign threat.
developments: He is the son of Jehu and succeeds him in Samaria (2 Kings 13:1)
He does evil in the sight of the Lord by following the sins of Jeroboam (2 Kings 13:2)
The Lord gives Israel into the hand of Hazael of Syria (2 Kings 13:3)
The northern kingdom is crushed by Aramean military dominance (2 Kings 13:3)
events.significance: His reign represents the lowest point of Israel's military power since the monarchy began.

events.title: Prayer and the Deliverer
events.summary: In his desperation, Jehoahaz turns to God for help, and his prayer is heard.
developments: Jehoahaz besought the Lord to relieve the Syrian oppression (2 Kings 13:4)
God sees how the King of Syria oppressed Israel (2 Kings 13:4)
The Lord gives Israel a 'savior' or deliverer (2 Kings 13:5)
The people are able to live in their tents again in peace (2 Kings 13:5)
events.significance: This event showed that God responds to even imperfect repentance from a wicked king.

events.title: Decimation of the Army
events.summary: The physical cost of the Syrian wars is recorded in the tiny remnant of Jehoahaz's forces.
developments: Syria leaves Jehoahaz only fifty horsemen and ten chariots (2 Kings 13:7)
He is left with only 10,000 footmen (2 Kings 13:7)
The King of Syria destroyed the rest and made them like dust (2 Kings 13:7)
Jehoahaz dies and is succeeded by his son Jehoash (2 Kings 13:9)
events.significance: The record emphasizes that Israel survived only by divine mercy, not military strength.

events.title: Succession in Judah
events.summary: A different Jehoahaz is made king by the people of Judah after the death of Josiah.
developments: The people of the land take Jehoahaz and anoint him (2 Kings 23:30)
He is twenty-three years old when he begins to reign (2 Kings 23:31)
He reigns for only three months in Jerusalem (2 Kings 23:31)
He does evil in the sight of the Lord as his fathers had done (2 Kings 23:32)
events.significance: This was the first Judean coronation under the shadow of Egyptian dominance.

events.title: Deposition and Exile
events.summary: Pharaoh Neco removes Jehoahaz from power and carries him as a captive to Egypt.
developments: Pharaoh puts him in bonds at Riblah (2 Kings 23:33)
The king of Egypt imposes a heavy tribute on the land (2 Kings 23:33)
Jehoahaz is taken to Egypt where he eventually dies (2 Kings 23:34)
Jeremiah prophesies that the nation should weep for the one who goes into exile (Jeremiah 22:10-12)
events.significance: Jehoahaz was the first king of Judah to die as a prisoner in a foreign land.

label: Jehoash
overview: Jehoash (also known as Joash) was the name of two prominent kings: the survivor of the Davidic line in Judah and a successful military ruler of Israel. Jehoash of Judah was hidden in the Temple as an infant to escape the purge of Athaliah and was later crowned at age seven through the courage of the high priest Jehoiada. His early reign was marked by significant Temple repairs, though his later years saw a spiritual decline after Jehoiada’s death. Jehoash of Israel was noted for his three victories over Syria, fulfilling the final prophecy of Elisha, and for his humiliating defeat of Judah, which resulted in the looting of Jerusalem. Both kings represent the complicated and often violent history of the divided kingdoms.

events.title: The Hidden Prince of Judah
events.summary: Infant Jehoash is rescued from a massacre and hidden in the Temple for six years.
developments: Athaliah attempts to destroy all the royal offspring (2 Kings 11:1)
Jehosheba, daughter of King Joram, steals Jehoash from among the princes (2 Kings 11:2)
He is hidden with his nurse in the house of the Lord (2 Kings 11:3)
Athaliah reigns over the land while the true king is concealed (2 Kings 11:3)
events.significance: His survival ensured the continuation of the Davidic line against Phoenician influence.

events.title: Coronation at Age Seven
events.summary: Jehoiada the priest organizes a coup to crown the young king and execute Athaliah.
developments: The guards surround the king to protect him (2 Kings 11:7-8)
Jehoiada puts the crown on him and gives him the testimony (2 Kings 11:12)
The people clap their hands and shout, 'Long live the king!' (2 Kings 11:12)
A covenant is made between the Lord, the king, and the people (2 Kings 11:17)
events.significance: This event restored the spiritual and political order of the southern kingdom.

events.title: Repairing the Temple
events.summary: Jehoash initiates a massive fundraising and construction project to restore the house of God.
developments: He commands the priests to collect money from the people (2 Kings 12:4)
The project is delayed until Jehoash takes personal oversight (2 Kings 12:6-7)
A chest with a hole in the lid is placed by the altar for contributions (2 Kings 12:9)
The money is paid directly to the masons and stonecutters (2 Kings 12:11-12)
events.significance: The Temple renovation was the primary positive legacy of his long reign.

events.title: Spiritual Decline and Murder
events.summary: After the death of Jehoiada, Jehoash turns to idolatry and executes his own cousin.
developments: The princes of Judah persuade him to serve Asherim and idols (2 Chronicles 24:17-18)
Zechariah, son of Jehoiada, rebukes the king for his transgression (2 Chronicles 24:20)
Jehoash commands the people to stone Zechariah in the Temple court (2 Chronicles 24:21)
Zechariah cries out, 'May the Lord see and avenge!' (2 Chronicles 24:22)
events.significance: This failure turned Jehoash into a symbol of a heart that forgets past kindness.

events.title: Accession of Jehoash of Israel
events.summary: In the north, a different Jehoash becomes king and visits the dying Elisha.
developments: He is the son of Jehoahaz and reigns for sixteen years in Samaria (2 Kings 13:10)
He visits Elisha and weeps, calling him 'the chariots of Israel' (2 Kings 13:14)
Elisha tells him to shoot the 'arrow of the Lord’s victory' (2 Kings 13:15-17)
The king strikes the ground three times, limiting his future victories (2 Kings 13:18-19)
events.significance: This Jehoash was the first northern king in decades to seek prophetic blessing.

events.title: The War of the Two Kings
events.summary: The two kings named Jehoash (or Joash/Amaziah) clash in a major civil war.
developments: Amaziah of Judah challenges Jehoash of Israel to battle (2 Kings 14:8)
Jehoash responds with a mocking parable about a thistle and a cedar (2 Kings 14:9)
Israel soundly defeats Judah at Beth-shemesh (2 Kings 14:12)
Jehoash of Israel captures Amaziah and plunders the Jerusalem Temple (2 Kings 14:13-14)
events.significance: The victory established the military superiority of the Northern Kingdom during this era.

events.title: Triple Victory over Syria
events.summary: Fulfilling Elisha's prophecy, Jehoash of Israel recovers lost territories from Ben-hadad.
developments: Hazael of Syria dies and his son Ben-hadad succeeds him (2 Kings 13:24)
Jehoash defeats Ben-hadad three times in battle (2 Kings 13:25)
He recovers the cities Hazael had taken from his father (2 Kings 13:25)
This restored some of the northern tribes' former glory (2 Kings 13:25)
events.significance: Jehoash’s military success was the turning point for northern resurgence.

events.title: Death and Succession
events.summary: Both kings die and are replaced by sons who would reach even greater heights.
developments: Jehoash of Judah is assassinated by his own servants (2 Kings 12:20-21)
His son Amaziah reigns in his place in Jerusalem (2 Kings 12:21)
Jehoash of Israel dies and is buried in Samaria (2 Kings 13:13)
His son Jeroboam II reigns and expands the northern empire (2 Kings 14:16, 23)
events.significance: The end of their reigns marked a transition to a final period of prosperity for both nations.

label: Jehoiachin
overview: Jehoiachin, also known as Jeconiah or Coniah, was the nineteenth king of Judah whose brief reign ended in the first major Babylonian deportation. After taking the throne at age eighteen during the siege of Jerusalem, he surrendered to Nebuchadnezzar to spare the city from total destruction. Jehoiachin spent thirty-seven years in a Babylonian prison, becoming the focus of a severe prophecy by Jeremiah that none of his descendants would sit on the throne of David. However, the book of Kings ends with the 'lifting up' of his head by the next Babylonian king, symbolizing the survival of the house of David. He is recorded as a direct ancestor of Jesus Christ, proving that God’s covenant mercy surpassed the legal curse on his life.

events.title: Accession to the Throne
events.summary: Jehoiachin becomes king of Judah after the death of his father Jehoiakim.
developments: He is eighteen years old when he begins to reign (2 Kings 24:8)
He reigns for only three months and ten days in Jerusalem (2 Kings 24:8)
He does evil in the sight of the Lord as his father did (2 Kings 24:9)
His mother is Nehushta, the daughter of Elnathan (2 Kings 24:8)
events.significance: He inherited a kingdom already doomed by the sins of his predecessors.

events.title: The Surrender to Babylon
events.summary: As Nebuchadnezzar's army surrounds the city, Jehoiachin chooses to give himself up.
developments: Nebuchadnezzar himself comes to the siege of Jerusalem (2 Kings 24:11)
Jehoiachin goes out to the King of Babylon with his household (2 Kings 24:12)
This surrender occurred in the eighth year of Nebuchadnezzar's reign (2 Kings 24:12)
He is the first Davidic king to be taken captive to a foreign land (2 Kings 24:12)
events.significance: His surrender marked the beginning of the Babylonian Captivity.

events.title: The First Great Deportation
events.summary: Babylon strips Jerusalem of its wealth and its best people alongside the king.
developments: Nebuchadnezzar carries off all the treasures of the Temple and palace (2 Kings 24:13)
He deports 10,000 captives, including all the officials and mighty men (2 Kings 24:14)
The craftsmen and smiths are also taken (2 Kings 24:14)
Only the poorest people of the land are left behind (2 Kings 24:14)
events.significance: This event removed the intellectual and military leadership of the nation.

events.title: The Prophetic Curse
events.summary: Jeremiah pronounces a devastating word against the future of Jehoiachin’s line.
developments: God calls him Coniah and says he will be hurled into a land he does not know (Jeremiah 22:24-28)
The land is told to 'Hear the word of the Lord!' regarding this man (Jeremiah 22:29)
God commands to 'Write this man down as childless' (Jeremiah 22:30)
He is told no man of his offspring shall prosper on the throne of David (Jeremiah 22:30)
events.significance: The prophecy appeared to legally end the Davidic monarchy forever.

events.title: The Release from Prison
events.summary: After thirty-seven years in captivity, Jehoiachin is miraculously elevated by a new Babylonian king.
developments: Evil-merodach becomes king of Babylon (2 Kings 25:27)
He releases Jehoiachin from prison on the twenty-seventh day of the twelfth month (2 Kings 25:27)
He speaks kindly to him and changes his prison clothes (2 Kings 25:28-29)
Jehoiachin receives a regular allowance from the king for the rest of his life (2 Kings 25:30)
events.significance: The book of Kings ends with this scene to show that the Davidic line still had a future.

events.title: Father of the Restoration
events.summary: Jehoiachin's descendants become the leaders of the return from exile.
developments: He fathers seven sons while in Babylon, including Shealtiel (1 Chronicles 3:17-18)
His grandson Zerubbabel leads the first group of exiles back to Jerusalem (Ezra 2:1-2)
Matthew includes 'Jechonias' (Jehoiachin) in the genealogy of Jesus (Matthew 1:11-12)
The genealogy proves that the Messiah comes from the very line that was once cursed (Matthew 1:12)
events.significance: Jehoiachin’s story highlights how God's grace overcomes historical and legal judgment.

label: Jehoiada
overview: Jehoiada was a righteous and courageous high priest who served as the spiritual and political savior of Judah during its most precarious era. Married to Princess Jehosheba, he was responsible for hiding the infant King Joash in the Temple for six years, protecting the last heir of David from the murderous Queen Athaliah. Jehoiada eventually organized a successful military coup to restore the boy to the throne, leading a national movement to purge Baal worship from Jerusalem. For his unwavering faithfulness and for acting as a godly mentor to the young king, Jehoiada was honored upon his death with a burial among the kings of Judah, a unique distinction for a priest.

events.title: Guardian of the Last Heir
events.summary: Jehoiada and his wife hide the infant Joash in the Temple to save the line of David.
developments: Athaliah attempts to destroy all the royal offspring (2 Kings 11:1)
Jehosheba, daughter of King Joram, hides the baby (2 Kings 11:2)
Jehoiada uses his authority as high priest to keep the child in the sanctuary (2 Kings 11:3)
Joash remains hidden with them for six years (2 Kings 11:3)
events.significance: Jehoiada was the physical protector of the Messianic promise during its most vulnerable moment.

events.title: The Coronation Plot
events.summary: In the seventh year, Jehoiada secretly organizes the military to reveal the true king.
developments: He summons the captains of the Carites and the guards (2 Kings 11:4)
He makes a covenant with them and shows them the king's son (2 Kings 11:4)
He arms the guards with the spears and shields of King David (2 Kings 11:10)
He places the crown and the testimony on the young Joash (2 Kings 11:12)
events.significance: Jehoiada demonstrated a masterful integration of religious authority and military strategy.

events.title: The Execution of Athaliah
events.summary: Jehoiada presides over the removal and execution of the usurper queen.
developments: Athaliah enters the Temple and cries 'Treason!' (2 Kings 11:13-14)
Jehoiada commands that she not be killed in the house of the Lord (2 Kings 11:15)
He orders the captains to take her out of the ranks (2 Kings 11:15)
She is killed at the entrance of the horse gate to the palace (2 Kings 11:16)
events.significance: His leadership ended the only non-Davidic reign in Judah’s history.

events.title: National Spiritual Reform
events.summary: Jehoiada leads the people in a covenant of rededication and a purge of idols.
developments: He makes a covenant between the Lord, the king, and the people (2 Chronicles 23:16)
He leads the people to the house of Baal to tear it down (2 Chronicles 23:17)
He oversees the execution of Mattan, the priest of Baal (2 Chronicles 23:17)
He appoints the offices of the house of the Lord under the priests and Levites (2 Chronicles 23:18)
events.significance: Jehoiada restored the original spiritual identity of the nation.

events.title: Mentor to the King
events.summary: The high priest serves as the primary advisor to Joash, ensuring a period of righteousness.
developments: Joash did what was right in the eyes of the Lord all his days (2 Kings 12:2)
The text specifies this was because 'Jehoiada the priest instructed him' (2 Kings 12:2)
He assists the king in organizing the finances for Temple repairs (2 Kings 12:4-9)
His presence ensured the king remained faithful to the Law (2 Kings 12:2)
events.significance: Jehoiada was the moral anchor for the southern kingdom during his lifetime.

events.title: Honored Death and Burial
events.summary: Jehoiada dies at a very old age and is given the burial of a king.
developments: He lived to the age of 130 years (2 Chronicles 24:15)
He is buried in the City of David among the kings (2 Chronicles 24:16)
The reason for this honor was that he had done 'good in Israel toward God and his house' (2 Chronicles 24:16)
His death is immediately followed by the spiritual decline of King Joash (2 Chronicles 24:17-18)
events.significance: His burial remains the highest national tribute ever paid to a priest in Israel.

label: Jehoiakim
overview: Jehoiakim, originally named Eliakim, was the eighteenth king of Judah whose reign was defined by his rebellion against both God and the Babylonian Empire. Appointed by Pharaoh Neco as an Egyptian vassal, he was a cruel and extravagant ruler who taxed his people heavily and built a luxurious palace using forced labor. Jehoiakim famously showed his contempt for God’s Word by cutting up and burning a scroll of Jeremiah’s prophecies. His reign was the catalyst for the final Babylonian invasions of Judah. He died in disgrace during the first siege of Jerusalem, with Jeremiah prophesying that he would be buried with the 'burial of a donkey.'

events.title: Accession as an Egyptian Vassal
events.summary: Pharaoh Neco installs Jehoiakim as king after deposing his brother Jehoahaz.
developments: The king of Egypt changes Eliakim's name to Jehoiakim (2 Kings 23:34)
He is twenty-five years old when he begins to reign (2 Kings 23:36)
He reigns for eleven years in Jerusalem (2 Kings 23:36)
He taxes the land heavily to pay the silver and gold demanded by Pharaoh (2 Kings 23:35)
events.significance: His reign began in total political subjugation to a foreign power.

events.title: Cruelty and Social Injustice
events.summary: The prophets denounce Jehoiakim for his greed and his oppression of the poor.
developments: He builds a great house by 'unrighteousness' and 'injustice' (Jeremiah 22:13)
He forces his neighbors to serve him for no wages (Jeremiah 22:13)
His eyes and heart are set only on dishonest gain and shedding innocent blood (Jeremiah 22:17)
He executes the prophet Uriah for speaking against the city (Jeremiah 26:20-23)
events.significance: Jehoiakim embodied the total reversal of his father Josiah's reforms.

events.title: Submission to Babylon
events.summary: Nebuchadnezzar invades Judah, forcing Jehoiakim to switch his allegiance from Egypt to Babylon.
developments: Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon comes up in his days (2 Kings 24:1)
Jehoiakim becomes his servant for three years (2 Kings 24:1)
After three years, he turns and rebels against Babylon (2 Kings 24:1)
The Lord sends bands of Chaldeans, Syrians, and Moabites to destroy Judah (2 Kings 24:2)
events.significance: This political instability led to the first phase of the Babylonian Exile.

events.title: Burning the Holy Scroll
events.summary: In his most infamous act, Jehoiakim destroys the written word of God.
developments: Jeremiah dictates his prophecies to Baruch to be read in the Temple (Jeremiah 36:4-10)
The scroll is brought before the king while he sits in his winter house (Jeremiah 36:21-22)
As each section is read, the king cuts it with a penknife and throws it into the fire (Jeremiah 36:23)
He and his servants show no fear and do not tear their clothes (Jeremiah 36:24)
events.significance: The act was a symbolic and literal rejection of divine authority.

events.title: Disgraceful End
events.summary: Jehoiakim dies during the Babylonian counter-attack, fulfilling a grim prophecy.
developments: Jeremiah predicts he will have the 'burial of a donkey' (Jeremiah 22:19)
The prophecy says his dead body will be cast out to the heat of the day and frost of night (Jeremiah 36:30)
Jehoiakim slept with his fathers (2 Kings 24:6)
His son Jehoiachin reigned in his place amidst the final siege (2 Kings 24:6)
events.significance: His life ended in national catastrophe and personal dishonor.

label: Jehonadab
overview: Jehonadab, also known as Jonadab, was the son of Rechab and a man of intense spiritual and moral conviction who lived during the reign of King Jehu. Known for his fierce opposition to Baal worship, he joined Jehu in his bloody purge of the house of Ahab, personally assisting in the mass execution of the priests of Baal. Jehonadab is most famous for establishing a strict ancestral code for his family, commanding his descendants to never drink wine, build houses, or plant vineyards. Centuries later, the prophet Jeremiah held up the 'Rechabites' as a model of faithfulness because they had strictly obeyed Jehonadab’s commands for generations, unlike Israel’s disobedience to God.

events.title: The Alliance with Jehu
events.summary: Jehonadab meets the new King Jehu to support his mission to destroy the house of Ahab.
developments: Jehu finds Jehonadab son of Rechab coming to meet him (2 Kings 10:15)
Jehu asks, 'Is your heart as true to mine as mine is to yours?' (2 Kings 10:15)
Jehonadab replies, 'It is' (2 Kings 10:15)
Jehu gives him his hand and pulls him into the royal chariot (2 Kings 10:15)
events.significance: His support gave Jehu's revolution immediate spiritual and tribal legitimacy.

events.title: The Purge of Baal
events.summary: Jehonadab accompanies Jehu into the temple of Baal to oversee the final destruction of the cult.
developments: Jehu says, 'Come with me and see my zeal for the Lord' (2 Kings 10:16)
They enter the house of Baal together (2 Kings 10:23)
Jehonadab helps identify the worshippers of Baal to ensure no servants of the Lord are present (2 Kings 10:23)
He witnesses the slaughter of the 80 priests of Baal (2 Kings 10:24-25)
events.significance: He acted as the witness and assistant to the total removal of Baalism from Israel.

events.title: The Family Mandate
events.summary: Jehonadab establishes a strict nomadic and ascetic lifestyle for all his future descendants.
developments: He commands his sons, 'You shall not drink wine, neither you nor your sons forever' (Jeremiah 35:6)
He forbids them from building houses or sowing seed (Jeremiah 35:7)
He commands them to live in tents all their days (Jeremiah 35:7)
The purpose was that they might live long in the land where they sojourned (Jeremiah 35:7)
events.significance: This was a radical cultural statement against the decadence of the monarchy era.

events.title: Generational Faithfulness
events.summary: Centuries later, the house of Rechab is tested by the prophet Jeremiah and found to be faithful.
developments: Jeremiah brings the Rechabites into the Temple and offers them wine (Jeremiah 35:2-5)
They refuse, citing the command of Jehonadab given nearly 300 years earlier (Jeremiah 35:6-10)
God uses their obedience to a human father to shame Israel's disobedience (Jeremiah 35:12-16)
God promises that Jehonadab's line will always have a man to stand before Him (Jeremiah 35:19)
events.significance: Jehonadab’s legacy provided a permanent biblical illustration of the power of ancestral integrity.

label: Jehoram
overview: Jehoram (also known as Joram) was the name of two contemporary kings whose reigns brought the kingdoms of Israel and Judah into a disastrous spiritual and political alliance. Jehoram of Judah, the son of Jehoshaphat, married the daughter of Ahab and Jezebel, leading Judah into intense idolatry and murdering his own brothers. His reign ended in military humiliation and a horrific terminal illness. Jehoram of Israel, the last of the house of Omri, struggled through numerous wars with Syria and the Moabite rebellion, eventually being assassinated by Jehu. Both kings represent the dark peak of the house of Ahab’s influence and the eventual violent fulfillment of God’s judgment against that dynasty.

events.title: Accession and Fratricide
events.summary: Jehoram of Judah begins his reign by eliminating all his royal brothers to secure his throne.
developments: He is the firstborn son of Jehoshaphat (2 Chronicles 21:3)
As soon as he had established himself, he killed all his brothers with the sword (2 Chronicles 21:4)
He also executed some of the other princes of Israel (2 Chronicles 21:4)
He married Athaliah, the daughter of Ahab (2 Chronicles 21:6)
events.significance: This act introduced a level of internal violence previously unseen in the Davidic line.

events.title: Jehoram of Israel and the Moabite War
events.summary: In the north, Jehoram succeeds his brother Ahaziah and attempts to retake Moab.
developments: Mesha, King of Moab, rebels after Ahab’s death (2 Kings 3:4-5)
Jehoram forms a coalition with Judah and Edom (2 Kings 3:6-9)
The army nearly dies of thirst in the wilderness (2 Kings 3:9)
Elisha provides a miraculous supply of water but prophesies a bittersweet victory (2 Kings 3:16-19)
events.significance: His reign was marked by constant struggle to maintain the Omride empire.

events.title: The Letter from Elijah
events.summary: Jehoram of Judah receives a written prophecy of judgment from Elijah for his wickedness.
developments: A letter arrives from the prophet Elijah condemning his idolatry (2 Chronicles 21:12)
He is rebuked for leading Judah into 'whoring' as the house of Ahab did (2 Chronicles 21:13)
Elijah predicts a great plague on his people, family, and possessions (2 Chronicles 21:14)
He is told he will suffer a severe intestinal disease (2 Chronicles 21:15)
events.significance: This was the only recorded interaction between Elijah and a king of Judah.

events.title: Rebellion and Raid
events.summary: Jehoram of Judah loses control of his vassals and suffers a devastating palace raid.
developments: Edom revolts against Judean rule and sets up its own king (2 Kings 8:20)
Philistines and Arabians invade and plunder the king’s house (2 Chronicles 21:16-17)
They carry away all the king's sons and wives except Jehoahaz (2 Chronicles 21:17)
The kingdom shrinks significantly in size and power (2 Kings 8:22)
events.significance: The military losses were seen as the direct result of Jehoram’s abandonment of God.

events.title: The Death of Jehoram of Judah
events.summary: He dies in extreme pain from the predicted disease and is buried without honor.
developments: The Lord struck him with an incurable bowel disease (2 Chronicles 21:18)
After two years, his bowels came out because of the disease (2 Chronicles 21:19)
The text says he 'departed with no one's regret' (2 Chronicles 21:20)
He was buried in the City of David but not in the tombs of the kings (2 Chronicles 21:20)
events.significance: His ignoble end was the definitive biblical example of a cursed reign.

events.title: The Assassination of Jehoram of Israel
events.summary: The last king of Ahab's house is executed by Jehu at the vineyard of Naboth.
developments: Jehoram is wounded in battle with the Syrians at Ramoth-gilead (2 Kings 9:14-15)
Jehu rides to Jezreel and meets Jehoram in the field (2 Kings 9:21)
Jehoram asks 'Is it peace, Jehu?' and Jehu rebukes him for Jezebel's sorceries (2 Kings 9:22)
Jehu draws his bow and strikes Jehoram through the heart (2 Kings 9:24)
events.significance: His death marked the total and violent collapse of the Omride dynasty in Israel.

label: Jehoshaphat
overview: Jehoshaphat was the fourth king of Judah and is remembered as one of the nation's most godly reformers. He dedicated his thirty-five-year reign to purging the land of high places and sent officials throughout the kingdom to teach the Book of the Law to the people. While he was a powerful military leader who fortified Judah's defenses, his primary weakness was a tendency to form dangerous political and marital alliances with the wicked house of Ahab in the northern kingdom. Despite this, his faith remained steadfast during crises, most notably when he led the nation in prayer against a massive foreign coalition and saw God destroy the enemy without a fight. Jehoshaphat’s legacy is that of a king who sought the Lord with all his heart, though his compromises left a complex impact on his descendants.

events.title: Accession and Initial Reforms
events.summary: Jehoshaphat succeeds his father Asa and immediately strengthens the kingdom's spiritual and physical state.
developments: He becomes king at age thirty-five and reigns twenty-five years (2 Chronicles 20:31)
The Lord was with him because he walked in the earlier ways of David (2 Chronicles 17:3)
He removes the high places and the Asherim from Judah (2 Chronicles 17:6)
He sends princes and Levites to teach the Book of the Law in all cities (2 Chronicles 17:7-9)
events.significance: This was the first systematic effort to educate the entire population in the Scriptures.

events.title: The Alliance with Ahab
events.summary: Jehoshaphat makes a marital alliance with the northern kingdom and joins Ahab in a disastrous war.
developments: He joins his house to Ahab by marriage (2 Chronicles 18:1)
He agrees to go to war at Ramoth-gilead but insists on seeking the Lord first (2 Chronicles 18:3-4)
He witnesses the prophecy of Micaiah against the 400 false prophets (2 Chronicles 18:12-27)
Jehoshaphat is nearly killed in battle when mistaken for Ahab (2 Chronicles 18:29-32)
events.significance: This compromise brought Judah into unnecessary danger and sparked a prophetic rebuke.

events.title: The Prophet's Rebuke and Legal Reform
events.summary: After the war, Jehu the seer rebukes the king for helping the wicked, leading to further reforms.
developments: Jehu asks, 'Should you help the wicked and love those who hate the Lord?' (2 Chronicles 19:2)
Jehoshaphat returns to Jerusalem and sets the people's hearts toward God (2 Chronicles 19:4)
He appoints judges in every fortified city of Judah (2 Chronicles 19:5)
He charges them to judge for the Lord without partiality or bribes (2 Chronicles 19:6-7)
events.significance: He transformed the judicial system of Judah into a godly administration.

events.title: Victory Through Prayer
events.summary: Faced with a massive invasion from Moab and Ammon, Jehoshaphat leads the nation in total reliance on God.
developments: He proclaims a fast throughout all Judah (2 Chronicles 20:3)
He prays a public prayer in the Temple, acknowledging 'we are powerless' (2 Chronicles 20:5-12)
The Spirit falls on Jahaziel, who declares the battle is God's (2 Chronicles 20:14-17)
The king appoints singers to lead the army with songs of praise (2 Chronicles 20:21)
events.significance: This event remains the definitive biblical example of spiritual warfare and national deliverance.

events.title: The Valley of Blessing
events.summary: God causes the enemy armies to destroy each other, leaving Judah with immense spoil.
developments: The Lord sets an ambush, and the invaders slaughter one another (2 Chronicles 20:22-23)
Judah spends three days collecting more spoil than they could carry (2 Chronicles 20:25)
They gather in the Valley of Beracah (Blessing) to praise the Lord (2 Chronicles 20:26)
The fear of God falls on all neighboring kingdoms as a result (2 Chronicles 20:29)
events.significance: The victory secured long-term peace and prosperity for the kingdom.

events.title: Final Failure and Legacy
events.summary: Jehoshaphat enters one last commercial alliance with the north that ends in failure.
developments: He partners with Ahaziah of Israel to build a fleet of trading ships (2 Chronicles 20:35-36)
Eliezer prophesies that because he joined the wicked, God will destroy the work (2 Chronicles 20:37)
The ships are wrecked at Ezion-geber and never sail (1 Kings 22:48)
Jehoshaphat dies and is buried in the City of David with his fathers (2 Chronicles 21:1)
events.significance: His life ended with a reminder that even godly leaders must remain vigilant against compromise.

label: Jehovah
overview: Jehovah is an English vocalization of the Tetragrammaton, the four-letter sacred name of God (YHWH) revealed to Moses at the burning bush. Derived from the Hebrew verb meaning 'to be,' the name signifies God's eternal, self-existent, and unchangeable nature. In most English translations, the name is rendered as 'the LORD' out of reverence for the Jewish tradition of not pronouncing the name aloud. Jehovah is the name associated with God’s covenant-keeping faithfulness, His presence with His people, and His role as the absolute Sovereign over history. In the biblical narrative, this name distinguishes the true God of Israel from the nameless and powerless idols of the surrounding nations.

events.title: Revelation at the Bush
events.summary: God reveals His personal name to Moses, establishing His eternal presence.
developments: Moses asks God for His name to tell the Israelites (Exodus 3:13)
God declares: 'I AM WHO I AM' (Exodus 3:14)
He identifies Himself as 'The Lord (YHWH), the God of your fathers' (Exodus 3:15)
He states this is His name forever, a memorial for all generations (Exodus 3:15)
events.significance: This revelation provided the foundation for Israel's unique monotheistic identity.

events.title: The Covenant Name
events.summary: God explains that the patriarchs knew Him as God Almighty, but He is now revealing the full weight of His name YHWH.
developments: God says He appeared to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob as El Shaddai (Exodus 6:3)
He states that by His name 'The Lord' (YHWH) He was not fully known to them (Exodus 6:3)
He links the name to the promise of delivering Israel from Egypt (Exodus 6:6-7)
The name becomes the guarantee of the Exodus (Exodus 6:2-8)
events.significance: Jehovah became the active, 'performing' name of God in the deliverance of the nation.

events.title: The Third Commandment
events.summary: The Law establishes the absolute sanctity and reverence required for God's name.
developments: God commands, 'You shall not take the name of the Lord your God in vain' (Exodus 20:7)
The penalty is stated: the Lord will not hold him guiltless (Exodus 20:7)
This led to the later tradition of replacing the name with 'Adonai' (Lord) in speech (Exodus 20:7)
The name was to be honored as an extension of God’s own person (Leviticus 24:16)
events.significance: The name was treated as a holy and powerful object of worship.

events.title: Proclamation of Character
events.summary: God passes before Moses and proclaims the internal meaning of His name.
developments: The Lord descends in the cloud and stands with Moses (Exodus 34:5)
He proclaims the name: 'The Lord, the Lord' (YHWH, YHWH) (Exodus 34:6)
He defines the name as merciful, gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in love (Exodus 34:6)
He emphasizes His justice andHis clearing of the guilty (Exodus 34:7)
events.significance: This defined Jehovah not just as a power, but as a moral and relational Being.

events.title: The Priestly Blessing
events.summary: God instructs the priests to 'put His name' upon the people through a specific benediction.
developments: The Lord tells Moses how Aaron should bless the people (Numbers 6:22-23)
The blessing invokes the name of the Lord three times (Numbers 6:24-26)
God says, 'So shall they put my name upon the people' (Numbers 6:27)
The presence of the name is equated with the bestowal of peace (Numbers 6:27)
events.significance: The name Jehovah served as the spiritual 'seal' of protection for the nation.

events.title: Victory in the Name
events.summary: David defeats Goliath by relying solely on the authority and power of the name of Jehovah.
developments: David tells the giant he comes in the name of the Lord of hosts (1 Samuel 17:45)
He contrasts the giant’s physical weapons with the spiritual weight of the name (1 Samuel 17:45)
He declares the battle belongs to the Lord (1 Samuel 17:47)
The victory proves that 'there is a God in Israel' (1 Samuel 17:46)
events.significance: The name of God was the primary offensive weapon of the faithful.

events.title: Personal and Sovereign Title
events.summary: The Psalms celebrate Jehovah as the personal Shepherd and the supreme Ruler of the earth.
developments: David declares, 'The Lord (Jehovah) is my shepherd' (Psalm 23:1)
The psalmist asks God to judge the nations so they know His name is the Lord (Psalm 83:18)
He is called the 'Most High over all the earth' (Psalm 83:18)
His name is described as a 'strong tower' where the righteous run and are safe (Proverbs 18:10)
events.significance: The name became the center of personal devotion and national security.

label: Jehu
overview: Jehu, the son of Jehoshaphat and grandson of Nimshi, was a military commander whom God raised up to execute judgment on the house of Ahab. Anointed king of Israel by a messenger of Elisha, Jehu carried out a swift and bloody purge, assassinating King Joram of Israel, King Ahaziah of Judah, and the notorious Queen Jezebel. He is best remembered for his 'zeal for the Lord,' which culminated in his deceptive and total destruction of the worshippers of Baal in Samaria.
